aboveboard.definition: In open sight; without trick, concealment, or deception.

abysmal.definition: Pertaining to, or resembling an abyss.

acme.definition: The top or highest point; pinnacle; culmination.

affable.definition: Receiving others kindly and conversing with them in a free and friendly manner; friendly, courteous, sociable.

affluent.example: They were affluent, but aspired to true wealth.

altruism.definition: Regard for others, both natural and moral without regard for oneself; devotion to the interests of others; brotherly kindness.

analogous.definition: Having analogy; corresponding to something else; bearing some resemblance or proportion (often followed by "to".)

animosity.definition: Violent hatred leading to active opposition; active enmity; energetic dislike.

antedate.definition: To occur before an event or time; to exist further back in time.

appreciable.definition: Large enough to be estimated; perceptible; considerable.

apprehension.definition: The physical act of seizing or taking hold of (something); seizing.

archaic.definition: Of or characterized by antiquity; old-fashioned, quaint, antiquated.

ascendancy.definition: The process or period of one's ascent

ascribe.definition: To attribute a cause or characteristic to someone or something.
ascribe.example: One may ascribe these problems to the federal government; however, at this stage it is unclear what caused them.

assail.definition: To attack with harsh words or violent force (also figuratively).

assuage.definition: To lessen the intensity of, to mitigate or relieve (hunger, emotion, pain etc.).

augment.definition: To increase; to make larger or supplement.
augment.example: The money from renting out a spare room can augment a salary.

autonomously.definition: In an autonomous or self-governing manner.

avarice.definition: Excessive or inordinate desire of gain; greed for wealth

avert.example: To avert the eyes from an object.

avid.definition: Enthusiastic; keen; eager; showing great interest in something or desire to do something

badger.definition: To pester, to annoy persistently; press.

banish.definition: (heading) To send someone away and forbid that person from returning.

begrudge.definition: To grudge about or over; be envious or covetous.

belittle.definition: To knowingly say that something is smaller or less important than it actually is, especially as a way of showing contempt or deprecation.

besiege.definition: To beset or surround with armed forces for the purpose of compelling to surrender, to lay siege to, beleaguer.

besmirch.example: The newspaper was on a campaign to besmirch the actor.

boon.example: Anaesthetics are a great boon to modern surgery.

boorish.definition: Behaving as a boor; rough in manners.

buttress.definition: To support something physically with, or as if with, a prop or buttress.

cadaverous.definition: Corpselike; hinting of death; imitating a cadaver.

catalyst.definition: A substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without being consumed in the process.
catalyst.example: Economic development and integration are working as a catalyst for peace.

cavalier.definition: Not caring enough about something important.

censor.definition: To review for, and if necessary to remove or suppress, content from books, films, correspondence, and other media which is regarded as objectionable (for example, obscene, likely to incite violence, or sensitive).

cerebral.definition: Of, or relating to the brain, cerebrum, or cerebral cortex.

checkered.definition: Divided into squares, or into light and dark patches.

chivalrous.definition: (of a man) Honourable, especially to women; gallant.

clemency.definition: The gentle or kind exercise of power; leniency, mercy; compassion in judging or punishing.

coalesce.definition: (of separate elements) To join into a single mass or whole.
coalesce.example: The droplets coalesced into a puddle.

cogent.definition: Reasonable and convincing; based on evidence.

collusion.definition: A secret agreement for an illegal purpose; conspiracy.

colossal.definition: Extremely large or on a great scale.

commendable.definition: Worthy of commendation; deserving praise; admirable, creditable, or meritorious.

complacent.definition: Uncritically satisfied with oneself or one's achievements; smug.

complementary.definition: Acting as a complement; making up a whole with something else.

conducive.definition: Tending to contribute to, encourage, or bring about some result.
conducive.example: A small, dark kitchen is not conducive to elaborate cooking.

consummate.definition: Complete in every detail, perfect, absolute.

contemptuous.definition: Showing contempt; expressing disdain; showing a lack of respect.
contemptuous.example: I don't know that guy, but he just gave me a contemptuous look.

contrite.definition: Sincerely penitent or feeling regret or sorrow, especially for one’s own actions; apologetic.

conundrum.definition: A difficult question or riddle, especially one using a play on words in the answer.

convivial.definition: Having elements of a feast or of entertainment, especially when it comes to eating and drinking, with accompanying festivity

convoluted.definition: Having numerous overlapping coils or folds; convolute.

cornucopia.definition: A goat's horn endlessly overflowing with fruit, flowers and grain; or full of whatever its owner wanted.
cornucopia.example: The store provided a veritable cornucopia of modern gadgets.

corroborate.definition: To confirm or support something with additional evidence; to attest or vouch for.

credence.definition: To give credence to; to believe.

credulity.definition: A willingness to believe in someone or something in the absence of reasonable proof; credulousness.

cumbersome.definition: Burdensome or hindering, as a weight or drag; vexatious
cumbersome.example: Cumbersome machines can endanger operators and slow down production.

dearth.definition: A period or condition when food is rare and hence expensive; famine.

debase.definition: To lower in character, quality, or value; to degrade.

debunk.definition: To discredit, or expose to ridicule the falsehood or the exaggerated claims of something.

decimation.definition: (strictly) The killing or punishment of every tenth person, usually by lot.

degrade.definition: To lower in value or social position.
degrade.example: Fred degrades himself by his behaviour.

demean.definition: To debase; to lower; to degrade.

demure.definition: (usually of women) Quiet, modest, reserved, sober, or serious.
demure.example: She is a demure young lady.

derisive.definition: Expressing or characterized by derision; mocking; ridiculing.
derisive.example: The critic's review of the film was derisive.

destitute.definition: (followed by the preposition "of") Lacking something; devoid

detrimental.example: Smoking tobacco can be detrimental to your health.

devolve.example: A discussion about politics may devolve into a shouting match.

dilapidated.definition: Having fallen into a state of disrepair or deterioration, especially through neglect.

diligent.definition: Performing with industrious concentration; hard-working and focused.

discord.definition: To disagree; to fail to agree or harmonize; clash.

discreet.definition: Respectful of privacy or secrecy; exercising caution in order to avoid causing embarrassment; quiet; diplomatic.

discriminate.definition: Having the difference marked; distinguished by certain tokens.

disenfranchise.definition: To deprive someone of a franchise, generally their right to vote

disparate.definition: Composed of inherently different or distinct elements; incongruous.

docile.definition: Ready to accept instruction or direction; obedient; subservient.

eccentric.definition: Not at or in the centre; away from the centre.

egotist.definition: A person who talks excessively about himself or herself.

eke.definition: Chiefly in the form eke out: to add to, to augment; to increase; to lengthen.

elude.definition: To evade, or escape from someone or something, especially by using cunning or skill

embellish.definition: To make more beautiful and attractive; to decorate.

embroiled.definition: To draw into a situation; to cause to be involved.

empathetic.definition: Showing empathy for others, and recognizing their feelings; empathic

endemic.definition: Native to a particular area or culture; originating where it occurs.

enmity.definition: The quality of being an enemy; hostile or unfriendly disposition.

entice.definition: To lure; to attract by arousing desire or hope.

enumerate.definition: To specify each member of a sequence individually in incrementing order.

err.example: He erred in his calculations, and made many mistakes.

errant.definition: Straying from the proper course or standard, or outside established limits.
errant.example: We ran down the street in pursuit of the errant dog.

erratic.example: Henry has been getting erratic scores on his tests: 40% last week, but 98% this week.

euphoria.definition: An excited state of joy; a feeling of intense happiness.

evasive.definition: Tending to avoid speaking openly or making revelations about oneself.

evenhanded.definition: Fair and having no partiality; unbiased; just.

excruciating.definition: Causing great pain or anguish, agonizing

exemplify.definition: To show or illustrate by example.

extenuating.definition: That lessens the seriousness of something by providing an excuse.

facetious.definition: Treating serious issues with (often deliberately) inappropriate humour; flippant.

fete.definition: (usually in the passive) To celebrate (a person).

fickle.definition: Quick to change one’s opinion or allegiance; insincere; not loyal or reliable.

finagle.definition: To obtain, arrange, or achieve by indirect, complicated and/or intensive efforts.

inadvertent.definition: Not intentional; not on purpose; not conscious.

inarticulate.definition: (of speech) not articulated in normal words

incense.example: I think it would incense him to learn the truth.

incessant.definition: Without pause or stop; not ending, especially to the point of annoyance.

indignant.definition: Showing anger or indignation, especially at something unjust or wrong.

industrious.example: I was very industrious in my effort to learn unicycle riding.

inflammable.definition: Capable of burning; easily set on fire.

ingenuity.definition: The ability to solve difficult problems, often in original, clever, and inventive ways.

insipid.example: The diners were disappointed with the plain, insipid soup they were served.

insolvent.definition: Unable to pay one's bills as they fall due.

irresolute.definition: Undecided or unsure how to act

jargon.definition: To utter jargon; to emit confused or unintelligible sounds.

malady.definition: Any ailment or disease of the body; especially, a lingering or deep-seated disorder.

malevolent.definition: Having or displaying ill will; wishing harm on others

malleable.definition: Able to be hammered into thin sheets; capable of being extended or shaped by beating with a hammer, or by the pressure of rollers.

malodorous.example: There were tons of malodorous garbage bags outside her house.

maxim.definition: A self-evident axiom or premise; a pithy expression of a general principle or rule.

meander.definition: To wind or turn in a course or passage; to be intricate.

misanthrope.definition: One who hates all mankind; one who hates the human race.

miscreant.definition: Lacking in conscience or moral principles; unscrupulous.

miser.definition: A person who hoards money rather than spending it; one who is cheap or extremely parsimonious.

misogynist.definition: Misogynistic: relating to or exhibiting misogyny.

morose.definition: Sullen, gloomy; showing a brooding ill humour.

morph.definition: (grammar) A physical form representing some morpheme in language. It is a recurrent distinctive sound or sequence of sounds.

obdurate.definition: Stubbornly persistent, generally in wrongdoing; refusing to reform or repent.

obliging.definition: Happy and ready to do favours for others.

obstinate.definition: Stubbornly adhering to an opinion, purpose, or course, usually with implied unreasonableness; persistent.

pertinent.definition: Important with regard to (a subject or matter); relevant

peruse.definition: To examine or consider with care.

pittance.definition: A small allowance of food and drink; a scanty meal.

redress.definition: To put in order again; to set right; to revise.

relegate.definition: Exile, banish, remove, or send away.

retract.example: An airplane retracts its wheels for flight.

rile.example: It riles me that she never closes the door after she leaves.

sanguine.definition: Having the colour of blood; blood red.

scintillating.definition: That scintillates with brief flashes of light; sparkling.

screed.definition: A piece or narrow strip cut or torn off from a larger whole; a shred.

sentimental.definition: Characterized by sentiment, sentimentality or excess emotion.

serendipity.definition: A combination of events which have come together by chance to make a surprisingly good or wonderful outcome.

slapdash.definition: Produced or carried out hastily; haphazard; careless.

smattering.definition: A superficial or shallow knowledge of a subject.

stolid.definition: Having or revealing little emotion or sensibility; dully or heavily stupid.

surly.example: The surly weather put us all in a bad mood.

tawdry.definition: (of clothing, appearance, etc.) Cheap and gaudy; showy.

taxing.definition: With respect to an experience: exhausting; draining.

telling.definition: Having force, or having a marked effect; weighty, effective.

travail.definition: Arduous or painful exertion; excessive labor, suffering, hardship.

tribulation.definition: Any adversity; a trying period or event.

tumult.definition: To make a tumult; to be in great commotion.

uncanny.definition: Strange, and mysteriously unsettling (as if supernatural); weird.

uncompromising.definition: Inflexible and unwilling to negotiate or make concessions.

unconscionable.definition: Not conscionable; unscrupulous and lacking principles or conscience.

unseemly.definition: Inconsistent with established standards of good form or taste.
unseemly.example: He was drunk and made some very unseemly comments.

urbane.definition: (of a man) Courteous, polite, refined, and suave.

variance.definition: The act of varying or the state of being variable.

veneer.example: to veneer a piece of furniture with mahogany

voracious.definition: Wanting or devouring great quantities of food.

wanton.definition: Undisciplined, unruly; not able to be controlled.

aberration.definition: The act of wandering; deviation from truth, moral rectitude; abnormal; divergence from the straight, correct, proper, normal, or from the natural state.

accolade.definition: To embrace or kiss in salutation.

acrimony.example: Her acrimony for her neighbors manifests itself with shouting and stomping.

adamant.definition: (said of people and their conviction) Firm; unshakeable; unyielding; determined.

aesthete.definition: Someone who cultivates an unusually high sensitivity to beauty, as in art or nature.

ambiguous.example: The politician was criticized for his ambiguous statements and lack of precision.

ambivalent.definition: Simultaneously experiencing or expressing opposing or contradictory feelings, beliefs, or motivations.
ambivalent.example: He has an ambivalent relationship towards his parents.

ameliorate.definition: To make better, or improve, something perceived to be in a negative condition.
ameliorate.example: They offered some compromises in an effort to ameliorate the situation.

amenable.definition: Willing to respond to persuasion or suggestions.

amorphous.definition: Lacking a definite form or clear shape.

anomalous.definition: Deviating from the normal; marked by incongruity or contradiction; aberrant or abnormal.

anomaly.example: He is an anomaly among his friends in that he's the only one who's unmarried.

antipathy.definition: A feeling of dislike (normally towards someone, less often towards something); repugnance or distaste.

antithetical.definition: Pertaining to antithesis, or opposition of words and sentiments; containing, or of the nature of, antithesis; contrasted.
antithetical.example: His wrong-headed beliefs are antithetical to everything we stand for as a community.

apathetic.definition: Void of feeling; not susceptible of deep emotion

apathy.definition: Lack of emotion or motivation; lack of interest or enthusiasm towards something; disinterest (in something).

apocryphal.definition: Of, or pertaining to, the Apocrypha.
apocryphal.example: Many scholars consider the stories of the monk Teilo to be apocryphal.

appease.definition: To make quiet; to calm; to reduce to a state of peace; to dispel (anger or hatred).

arduous.definition: Needing or using up much energy; testing powers of endurance.
arduous.example: The movement towards a peaceful settlement has been a long and arduous political struggle.

artful.definition: Cunning; tending toward indirect dealings; crafty.

askance.definition: Turned to the side, especially of the eyes.

audacious.definition: Showing willingness to take bold risks; recklessly daring.

audacity.definition: Insolent boldness, especially when imprudent or unconventional.
audacity.example: Somebody never pays his loans, yet he has the audacity to ask the bank for money.

auspicious.definition: Of good omen; indicating future success.

austere.definition: Grim or severe in manner or appearance

avaricious.definition: Actuated by avarice; extremely greedy for wealth or material gain; immoderately desirous of accumulating property.

banal.definition: Common in a boring way, to the point of being predictable; containing nothing new or fresh.

blatant.definition: Bellowing; disagreeably clamorous; sounding loudly and harshly.

bolster.definition: To brace, reinforce, secure, or support.

brazen.definition: Pertaining to, made of, or resembling brass (in color or strength).
brazen.example: She was brazen enough to deny stealing the handbag even though she was caught on closed-circuit television doing so.

bumbling.definition: Acting in a confused or ineffectual way; incompetent; showing little or no skill.

burgeon.example: Gradually, the town burgeoned into a thriving city.

calumny.definition: To make false accusations or levy false charges against a person with the intent to tarnish that person's reputation or standing; to calumniate.

capricious.definition: Impulsive and unpredictable; determined by chance, impulse, or whim
capricious.example: I almost died in a capricious winter storm.

castigate.definition: To punish or reprimand someone severely.

chastise.definition: To punish (someone), especially by corporal punishment.

chortle.definition: To laugh with a chortle or chortles.

circumscribe.definition: To draw a line around; to encircle.

circumvent.definition: To avoid or get around something; to bypass

commensurate.definition: Of a proportionate or similar measurable standard.

confound.example: Don't confound the situation by yelling.

duress.definition: To put under duress; to pressure.

eclectic.definition: Selecting a mixture of what appears to be best of various doctrines, methods or styles.

economical.definition: Careful with money so as not to spend too much; prudent; thrifty.

edifying.definition: That educates, informs, illuminates or instructs.

egregious.definition: Usually in a negative sense: conspicuous, exceptional, outstanding.
egregious.example: The student has made egregious errors on the examination.

elicit.definition: Elicited; drawn out; made real; open; evident.

elucidate.definition: To make clear; to clarify; to shed light upon.

eminent.example: His eminent good sense has been a godsend to this project.

engender.definition: To beget (of a man); to bear or conceive (of a woman).

ephemeral.definition: Lasting for a short period of time.

eradicate.definition: To pull up by the roots; to uproot.

erudite.definition: Learned, scholarly, with emphasis on knowledge gained from books.

eschew.definition: To avoid; to shun, to shy away from.

circumlocution.definition: A roundabout or indirect way of speaking; the use of more words than necessary to express an idea.

circumspect.example: Being aware of the danger of upsetting her audience, she was somewhat circumspect in her comments.

cistern.definition: A reservoir or tank for holding water, especially for catching and holding rainwater for later use.

civility.definition: Speech or behaviour that is fit for civil interactions; politeness, courtesy.

clandestine.definition: Done or kept in secret, sometimes to conceal an illicit or improper purpose.

clasp.definition: To take hold of; to grasp; to grab tightly.
clasp.example: They clasped hands and parted as friends.

cleft.definition: To syntactically separate a prominent constituent from the rest of the clause that concerns it, such as threat in "The threat which I saw but which he didn't see, was his downfall."

cliche.definition: To use a cliché; to make up a word or a name that sounds like a cliché.

clique.definition: To associate together in a clannish way; to act with others secretly to gain a desired end; to plot.

clog.definition: To block or slow passage through (often with 'up').

clot.definition: To form a clot or mass.

cloture.definition: To end legislative debate by this means.

clout.definition: To hit, especially with the fist.

cloying.example: The cloying fondness she displayed was what, in the end, drove me away.

clumsy.definition: Awkward, lacking coordination, not graceful, not dextrous.
clumsy.example: He's very clumsy. I wouldn't trust him with carrying the dishes.

coagulant.definition: That causes coagulation or that coagulates

coax.example: He coaxed the horse gently into the trailer.

coda.definition: A person born hearing to deaf parents.

coerce.definition: To restrain by force, especially by law or authority; to repress; to curb.

coeval.example: Anything coeval with that clock will fetch a hefty price!

cognizant.definition: Aware; fully informed; having understanding of a fact
cognizant.example: The defendant is cognizant that this is a serious charge.

colander.definition: A bowl-shaped kitchen utensil with holes in it used for draining food such as pasta.

collude.definition: To act in concert with; to conspire

colonnade.definition: A series of columns at regular intervals.

coma.definition: A state of unconsciousness from which one may not wake up, usually induced by some form of trauma.

combustible.example: Dumping fertilizer on top of whatever mysterious goop was in the storage tank created a combustible mix which caught fire.

comely.definition: (of a person) Pleasing or attractive to the eye.

comity.definition: Courtesy and considerate behaviour towards others; social harmony.

commencement.definition: The first existence of anything; act or fact of commencing
commencement.example: The time of Henry VII nearly coincides with the commencement of what is termed modern history.

commend.example: The schoolboy was commended for raising the alarm about the burning building.

committed.definition: Obligated by a pledge to some course of action.

commodious.example: Our house is much more commodious than our old apartment.

compendium.definition: A short, complete summary; an abstract.

complacency.definition: A feeling of contented self-satisfaction, especially when unaware of upcoming trouble.

compliant.definition: Willing to comply; submissive; willing to do what someone wants.
compliant.example: Tendon is compliant compared to bone.

compliment.definition: To pay a compliment (to); to express a favorable opinion (of).

compress.definition: To make smaller; to press or squeeze together, or to make something occupy a smaller space or volume.
compress.example: The force required to compress a spring varies linearly with the displacement.

compunction.definition: A pricking of conscience or a feeling of regret, especially one which is slight or fleeting.

concatenate.definition: Joined together as if in a chain.

concave.definition: Curved like the inner surface of a sphere or bowl

concerted.definition: Performed through a concert of effort; done by agreement or in combination.

conciliate.definition: To make calm and content, or regain the goodwill of; to placate.

concise.definition: Brief, yet including all important information

concord.definition: A state of agreement; harmony; union.

concur.definition: To unite or agree (in action or opinion); to have a common opinion; to coincide; to correspond.

condescending.definition: Assuming a tone of superiority, or a patronizing attitude.
condescending.example: Quit talking to me in that condescending tone! You always treat me like a child!

condign.definition: Fitting, appropriate, deserved, especially denoting punishment

condole.definition: To express sympathetic sorrow; to lament in sympathy (with someone on something).

condone.definition: To forgive, excuse or overlook (something that is considered morally wrong, offensive, or generally disliked).

conductor.definition: One who conducts or leads; a guide; a director.

confluence.definition: The place where two rivers, streams, or other continuously flowing bodies of water meet and become one, especially where a tributary joins a river.
confluence.example: We encountered an abandoned boat at the confluence of the Mississippi and Ohio Rivers.

congeal.definition: To change from a liquid to solid state perhaps by cold
congeal.example: We must act before opposition to our plans congeals.

congenial.definition: Having the same or very similar nature, personality, tastes, habits or interests.
congenial.example: The congenial bartender makes the Hog’s Head an inviting place to hang out during the weekends.

conjecture.definition: To guess; to venture an unproven idea.
conjecture.example: I do not know if it is true; I am simply conjecturing here.

connive.definition: Often followed by with: to secretly cooperate with another person or persons in order to commit a crime or other wrongdoing; to collude, to conspire.

connoisseur.definition: A specialist in a given field whose opinion is highly valued, especially in one of the fine arts or in matters of taste

conscientious.definition: Thorough, careful, or vigilant in one’s task performance.
conscientious.example: He was a thoughtful and conscientious worker.

conservatory.definition: Having the quality of preserving from loss, decay, or injury.

console.definition: A stand-alone cabinet designed to stand on the floor; especially, one that houses home entertainment equipment, such as a TV or stereo system.

constrain.definition: To force physically, by strong persuasion or pressuring; to compel; to oblige.

contagious.definition: (of a disease) Easily transmitted to others.
contagious.example: The flu was so contagious that everybody in town got sick!

contaminate.definition: To make something dangerous or toxic by introducing impurities or foreign matter.
contaminate.example: This water is contaminated. It isn't safe to drink.

contentious.definition: Marked by heated arguments or controversy.

contort.definition: To twist in a violent manner.

contravene.definition: To act contrary to an order; to fail to conform to a regulation or obligation.

contumacious.definition: Contemptuous of authority; willfully disobedient; rebellious.

convalesce.definition: To recover health and strength gradually after sickness or weakness.
convalesce.example: The patient convalesced for six months after his stroke.

conversant.definition: Closely familiar; current; having frequent interaction
conversant.example: She is equally conversant with Shakespeare and the laws of physics.

convex.definition: Curved or bowed outward like the outside of a bowl or sphere or circle

convoke.definition: To convene, to cause to assemble for a meeting.

convulsion.definition: An intense, paroxysmal, involuntary muscular contraction.
convulsion.example: Earthquakes and convulsions of nature shake Earth on a regular basis.

coquette.definition: To act as a flirt or coquet.

cordon.definition: A ribbon normally worn diagonally across the chest as a decoration or insignia of rank etc.

corona.definition: To surround with a luminous or crownlike ring as if by the solar corona.

coronation.definition: The act or solemnity of crowning a sovereign; the act of investing a prince with the insignia of royalty, on his succeeding to the sovereignty.

corrosive.definition: Eating away; having the power of gradually wearing, hanging, or destroying the texture or substance of a body; as the corrosive action of an acid.

corrugated.definition: Marked with parallel folds, ridges or furrows.

cosset.definition: To treat like a pet; to overly indulge.
cosset.example: The car cossets its occupants in comfort.

countenance.definition: To tolerate, support, sanction, patronise or approve of something.
countenance.example: The cruel punishment was countenanced by the government, although it was not officially legal.

counterfeit.definition: False, especially of money; intended to deceive or carry appearance of being genuine.
counterfeit.example: This counterfeit watch looks like the real thing, but it broke a week after I bought it.

countermand.definition: To revoke (a former command); to cancel or rescind by giving an order contrary to one previously given.
countermand.example: to countermand an order for goods

coven.definition: A formal group or assembly of witches.

covetous.definition: Extremely keen or desirous, especially to obtain and possess something belonging to someone else; avaricious.

cower.definition: To crouch or cringe, or to avoid or shy away from something, in fear.
cower.example: He'd be useless in war. He'd just cower in his bunker until the enemy came in and shot him, or until the war was over.

cozen.definition: To become cozy; (by extension) to become acquainted, comfortable, or familiar with.

cramped.definition: Uncomfortably restricted in size, or financially.

crass.definition: Coarse; crude; unrefined or insensitive; lacking discrimination

crave.definition: To desire strongly, so as to satisfy an appetite; to long or yearn for.

crease.definition: To make a crease in; to wrinkle.
crease.example: The bullet just creased his shoulder.

credulous.definition: Excessively ready to believe things; gullible.

creek.definition: A small inlet or bay, often saltwater, narrower and extending farther into the land than a cove; a recess in the shore of the sea, or of a river; the inner part of a port that is used as a dock for small boats.

crescendo.definition: To increase in intensity; to reach or head for a crescendo.
crescendo.example: The band crescendoed and then suddenly went silent.

crest.definition: Particularly with reference to waves, to reach a peak.

crestfallen.definition: Sad because of a recent disappointment.

cronyism.definition: Favoritism to friends without regard for their qualifications, especially by appointing them to political positions.

crook.definition: To bend, or form into a hook.
crook.example: He crooked his finger toward me.

croon.definition: To hum or sing softly or in a sentimental manner.

crouch.definition: To bend down; to stoop low; to stand close to the ground with legs bent, like an animal when waiting for prey, or someone in fear.
crouch.example: We crouched behind the low wall until the squad of soldiers had passed by.

crumple.definition: To rumple; to press into wrinkles by crushing together.

crutch.definition: To support on crutches; to prop up.

crux.definition: The basic, central, or essential point or feature.
crux.example: The crux of her argument was that the roadways needed repair before anything else could be accomplished.

cuisine.definition: A characteristic style of preparing food, often associated with a place of origin.
cuisine.example: French cuisine is considered to be one of the world's most refined and elegant styles of cooking.

culpable.definition: Meriting condemnation, censure or blame, especially as something wrong, harmful or injurious; blameworthy.
culpable.example: I am culpable for stealing your money.

cunning.definition: Sly; crafty; clever in surreptitious behaviour.

curator.definition: A person who manages, administers or organizes a collection, either independently or employed by a museum, library, archive or zoo.

curmudgeon.definition: An ill-tempered person full of stubborn ideas or opinions.
curmudgeon.example: John Doe's old age and stubborn aversion to new ideas make him a curmudgeon of a candidate.

cursory.example: Most junk mail requires only a cursory glance.

curt.definition: Brief or terse, especially to the point of being rude.

curtail.definition: To cut short the tail of an animal

cynic.example: the cynic, or Sothic, year; cynic cycle

dabble.definition: To make slightly wet or soiled by spattering or sprinkling a liquid (such as water, mud, or paint) on it; to bedabble.
dabble.example: The children sat on the dock and dabbled their feet in the water.

daguerreotype.definition: To make a photograph using this process, to make a daguerreotype (of).

dais.definition: A raised platform in a room for a high table, a seat of honour, a throne, or other dignified occupancy; a similar platform supporting a lectern, pulpit, etc., which may be used to speak from.

dally.definition: To waste time in trivial activities, or in idleness; to trifle.

damper.definition: In a state between dry and wet; moderately wet; moist.
damper.example: The lawn was still damp so we decided not to sit down.

dandy.example: That's all fine and dandy, but how much does it cost?

dank.example: The dank cave was chilly and spooky.

dappled.definition: Having a mottled or spotted skin or coat, dapple.

daredevil.example: The climatic scene of “Rebel without a Cause” is the group of very daredevil teens playing chicken.

dart.definition: A pointed missile weapon, intended to be thrown by the hand, for example a short lance or javelin

dawdle.definition: To spend time idly and unfruitfully, to waste time.
dawdle.example: to dawdle away the whole morning

debacle.definition: An event or enterprise that ends suddenly and disastrously, often with humiliating consequences.

debark.definition: To unload goods from an aircraft or ship.

debilitate.example: The American Dream suffered a debilitating effect after the subprime crisis.

decant.definition: To pour off (a liquid) gently, so as not to disturb the sediment.

decibel.definition: A common measure of sound intensity ratio that is one tenth of a bel on the logarithmic intensity scale. It is defined as dB = 10 log10(P1 / P2), where P1 and P2 are the relative powers of the sound.

decipher.definition: To decode or decrypt a code or cipher to plain text.

decode.definition: To convert from an encrypted form to plain text.

decrepit.definition: Weakened or worn out from age or wear.

defer.example: We're going to defer the decision until we have all the facts.

deference.example: The children treated their elders with deference.

defile.definition: To make unclean, dirty, or impure; soil; befoul.
defile.example: To urinate on someone's grave is an example of a way to defile it.

defoliant.definition: An agent used to defoliate plants.

deft.definition: Quick and neat in action; skillful.
deft.example: He assembled it in one fluid, deft motion.

defuse.definition: To remove the fuse from (a bomb, etc.).

dehydrate.definition: To lose or remove water; to dry

deify.definition: To make a god of (something or someone).

delirium.definition: A temporary mental state with a sudden onset, usually reversible, including symptoms of confusion, inability to concentrate, disorientation, anxiety, and sometimes hallucinations. Causes can include dehydration, drug intoxication, and severe infection.

delusion.definition: A false belief that is resistant to confrontation with actual facts.
delusion.example: It is far better to grasp the universe as it really is than to persist in delusion, however satisfying and reassuring.

deluge.example: Some areas were deluged with a month's worth of rain in 24 hours.

delve.definition: To dig the ground, especially with a shovel.

demagogue.definition: To speak or act in the manner of a demagogue; to speak about (an issue) in the manner of a demagogue.

demanding.definition: Requiring much endurance, strength, or patience.

demolition.definition: The process of demolishing or destroying buildings or other structures.

demoralize.definition: To destroy the morale of; to dishearten.

demotic.definition: Of or for the common people.

denouement.definition: (authorship, often used metaphorically) The conclusion or resolution of a plot.

dent.definition: To impact something, producing a dent.
dent.example: Copper is soft and dents easily.

denude.definition: To divest of all covering; to make bare or naked; to strip.
denude.example: He denuded himself of his clothes.

deplete.definition: To empty or unload, as the vessels of the human system, by bloodletting or by medicine.
deplete.example: Certain medications can deplete vitamin D.

deplore.definition: To bewail; to weep bitterly over; to feel sorrow for.
deplore.example: I deplore my neighbour for having lost his job.

deportation.definition: The act of deporting or exiling, or the state of being deported; banishment; transportation.

depose.definition: To put down; to lay down; to deposit; to lay aside; to put away.
depose.example: A deposed monarch may go into exile as pretender to the lost throne, hoping to be restored in a subsequent revolution.

depravity.definition: The state or condition of being depraved; moral debasement.

deprecate.definition: To belittle or express disapproval of.
deprecate.example: He deprecates any praise of his own merits.

depreciate.definition: To lessen in price or estimated value; to lower the worth of.

depressant.definition: (pharmaceutical effect) reducing functional or nervous activity.

deprivation.definition: The act of depriving, dispossessing, or bereaving; the act of deposing or divesting of some dignity.
deprivation.example: He was suffering from deprivation of sleep.

deracinate.definition: To pull up by the roots; to uproot; to extirpate.

derelict.definition: Abandoned, forsaken; given up by the natural owner or guardian; (of a ship) abandoned at sea, dilapidated, neglected; (of a spacecraft) abandoned in outer space.
derelict.example: There was a derelict ship on the island.

desiccate.definition: Having had moisture removed; dehydrated, dessicated.

despicable.definition: Fit or deserving to be despised; contemptible; mean

despise.definition: To regard with contempt or scorn.

despotic.definition: Of or pertaining to a despot or tyrant.
despotic.example: This despotic age of the empire ended in a revolt.

dessert.definition: A sweet confection served as the last course of a meal

desultory.definition: Jumping, or passing, from one thing or subject to another, without order, planning, or rational connection; lacking logical sequence.
desultory.example: He wandered round, cleaning up in a desultory way.

detach.definition: To take apart from; to take off.
detach.example: to detach the tag from a newly purchased garment

deterioration.definition: The process of making or growing worse, or the state of having grown worse.

detract.definition: To take away; to withdraw or remove.

detritus.definition: (chiefly geological) Pieces of rock broken off by ice, glacier, or erosion.

devoted.example: Bob and Sara are devoted to their children.

devout.definition: Devoted to religion or to religious feelings and duties; pious; extremely religious.
devout.example: devout sighs; devout eyes; a devout posture

dew.definition: Any moisture from the atmosphere condensed by cool bodies upon their surfaces.
dew.example: There was a heavy dew this morning.

diabolic.definition: Showing wickedness typical of a devil.

diaphanous.definition: Transparent or translucent; allowing light to pass through; capable of being seen through.

diatribe.definition: An abusive, bitter, attack or criticism: denunciation.
diatribe.example: The senator was prone to diatribes which could go on for more than an hour.

diehard.example: For a Roman Catholic teacher, he sure is a diehard fundamentalist.

diffuse.definition: To spread over or through as in air, water, or other matter, especially by fluid motion or passive means.

digit.definition: To point at or point out with the finger.

digress.definition: To step or turn aside; to deviate; to swerve; especially, to turn aside from the main subject of attention, or course of argument, in writing or speaking.

dilapidate.definition: To fall into ruin or disuse.

dilate.example: The eye doctor put drops in my eye to dilate the pupil so he could see the nerve better.

dilute.example: Clean the panel with a dilute, neutral cleaner.

din.definition: A loud noise; a cacophony or loud commotion.

dire.definition: Warning of bad consequences: ill-boding; portentous.

disabuse.definition: To free (someone) of a misconception or misapprehension; to unveil a falsehood held by (somebody).

disarm.definition: To deprive of weapons; to deprive of the means of attack or defense; to render defenseless.

disarray.definition: To throw into disorder; to break the array of.

disavow.definition: To strongly and solemnly refuse to own or acknowledge; to deny responsibility for, approbation of, and the like.
disavow.example: He was charged with embezzlement, but he disavows the crime.

discern.definition: To detect with the senses, especially with the eyes.
discern.example: He was too young to discern right from wrong.

disciple.definition: To convert (a person) into a disciple.

discombobulate.definition: To throw into a state of confusion; to befuddle or perplex.

discompose.definition: To destroy the composure of; to disturb or agitate.

disconcert.example: The emperor disconcerted the plans of his enemy.

discredit.definition: To harm the good reputation of a person; to cause an idea or piece of evidence to seem false or unreliable.
discredit.example: The candidate tried to discredit his opponent.

discrepancy.definition: An inconsistency between facts or sentiments.
discrepancy.example: They found a discrepancy between the first set of test results and the second, and they're still trying to figure out why.

discretionary.definition: Available at one's discretion; able to be used as one chooses; left to or regulated by one's own discretion or judgment.

discursive.definition: (of speech or writing) Tending to digress from the main point; rambling.

disdain.definition: To regard (someone or something) with strong contempt.

disengage.definition: To release or loosen from something that binds, entangles, holds, or interlocks.

disgorge.definition: To vomit or spew, to discharge.

disgruntle.definition: To make discontent or cross; to put in a bad temper.

disguise.definition: To change the appearance of (a person or thing) so as to hide, or to assume an identity.

disinclination.definition: The state of being disinclined; want of propensity, desire, or affection; slight aversion or dislike

disinfect.definition: To sterilize by the use of cleaning agent.

disjointed.example: The hours of his illness were disjointed and confusing as he drifted in and out of consciousness.

disinter.definition: To take out of the grave or tomb.

dismantle.definition: To divest, strip of dress or covering.

dismay.definition: To cause to feel apprehension; great sadness, or fear; to deprive of energy

disparage.definition: To match unequally; to degrade or dishonor.

disregard.definition: To ignore; pay no attention to.

dissect.definition: To study an animal's anatomy by cutting it apart; to perform a necropsy or an autopsy.

dissension.definition: An act of expressing dissent, especially spoken.

dissent.definition: To disagree; to withhold assent. Construed with from (or, formerly, to).

dissonance.definition: A harsh, discordant combination of sounds.

dissuade.definition: To convince not to try or do.
dissuade.example: Jane dissuaded Martha from committing suicide.

distal.definition: Remote from the point of attachment or origin.
distal.example: the distal end of a bone or muscle

distend.definition: To extend or expand, as from internal pressure; to swell

distill.definition: To subject a substance to distillation.

distraught.definition: Deeply hurt, saddened, or worried; distressed.
distraught.example: His distraught widow cried for days, feeling very alone.

ditty.definition: To sing; to warble a little tune.

diurnal.definition: Happening or occurring during daylight, or primarily active during that time.

diverge.definition: (of lines or paths) To run apart; to separate; to tend into different directions.
diverge.example: Both stories start out the same way, but they diverge halfway through.

divestiture.definition: The act of divesting, or something divested.

divulge.definition: To make public or known; to communicate to the public; to tell (information, especially a secret) so that it may become generally known
divulge.example: I will never divulge that secret to anyone.

dodder.definition: To shake or tremble as one moves, especially as of old age or childhood; to totter.

dodge.definition: To avoid (something) by moving suddenly out of the way.
dodge.example: He dodged traffic crossing the street.

doff.definition: (clothing) To remove or take off, especially of clothing.
doff.example: The rustics doffed their hats at the clergy.

dogged.definition: To pursue with the intent to catch.
dogged.example: The woman cursed him so that trouble would dog his every step.

doggerel.definition: Of a crude or irregular construction. (Originally applied to humorous verse, but now to verse lacking artistry or meaning.)

dogma.definition: An authoritative principle, belief or statement of opinion, especially one considered to be absolutely true and indisputable, regardless of evidence or without evidence to support it.
dogma.example: In the Catholic Church, new dogmas can only be declared by the pope after the extremely rare procedure ex cathedra to make them part of the official faith.

domicile.definition: To have a domicile in a particular place.
domicile.example: The answer depends on which state he was domiciled in at his death.

domination.definition: Area or situation which screens a person or thing from view.
domination.example: The soldiers took cover behind a ruined building.

don.definition: A university professor, particularly one at Oxford or Cambridge.

doodle.example: The bored student doodled a submarine in his notebook.

dormant.example: Grass goes dormant during the winter, waiting for spring before it grows again.

douse.definition: To plunge suddenly into water; to duck; to immerse.

downplay.definition: To de-emphasize; to present or portray as less important or consequential.
downplay.example: He would sometimes downplay his Princeton education by saying simply that he went to school in New Jersey.

doyen.definition: A commander in charge of ten men.

doze.definition: To sleep lightly or briefly; to nap, snooze.
doze.example: I didn’t sleep very well, but I think I may have dozed a bit.

drawl.definition: To drag on slowly and heavily; to while or dawdle away time indolently.

dreary.example: It had rained for three days straight, and the dreary weather dragged the townspeople's spirits down.

drench.definition: To soak, to make very wet.

drivel.definition: To have saliva drip from the mouth; to drool.

drizzle.example: We had planned a picnic for Joe's birthday, but it ended up drizzling all day.

effluvium.definition: A gaseous or vaporous emission, especially a foul-smelling one.

effrontery.example: We even had the effrontery to suggest that he should leave the country.

effusive.definition: Gushy; unrestrained, extravagant or excessive (in emotional expression).

egalitarian.definition: Characterized by social equality and equal rights for all people.

egoism.definition: The tendency to think selfishly with exclusive self-interest in mind.

elastic.definition: Capable of stretching; particularly, capable of stretching so as to return to an original shape or size when force is released.
elastic.example: The rope is somewhat elastic, so expect it to give when you pull on it.

eleemosynary.definition: Relating to charity, alms, or almsgiving.

elegy.definition: A mournful or plaintive poem; a funeral song; a poem of lamentation.

elephantine.definition: Of, pertaining to, or characteristic of elephants.

ellipsis.definition: A mark consisting of (in English) three periods, historically or more formally with spaces in between, before, and after them “ . . . ”, or more recently a single character “…” Ellipses are used to indicate that words have been omitted in a text or that they are missing or illegible.

elliptical.definition: In a shape of, or reminding of, an ellipse; oval.

embargo.definition: To impose an embargo on trading certain goods with another country.

embarrass.definition: To humiliate; to disrupt somebody's composure or comfort with acting publicly or freely; to disconcert; to abash
embarrass.example: The crowd's laughter and jeers embarrassed him.

embezzle.definition: To steal or misappropriate money that one has been trusted with, especially to steal money from the organisation for which one works.
synonyms: have one's hand in the till

embolden.definition: To render (someone) bolder or more courageous.

emboss.definition: To mark or decorate with a raised design or symbol.
emboss.example: The papers weren't official until the seal had been embossed on them.

embroider.definition: To stitch a decorative design on fabric with needle and thread of various colours.

emigrate.definition: To leave the country in which one lives, especially one's native country, in order to reside elsewhere.

eminence.definition: Someone of high rank, reputation or social status.

enamel.definition: An opaque, glassy coating baked onto metal or ceramic objects.

encomium.definition: Warm praise, especially a formal expression of such praise; a tribute.

encumber.definition: To load down something with a burden

enfranchise.definition: To grant the franchise to an entity, specifically:

engross.definition: To write (a document) in large, aesthetic, and legible lettering; to make a finalized copy of.
engross.example: She seems to be completely engrossed in that book.

enigma.definition: Something or someone puzzling, mysterious or inexplicable.

ennoble.definition: To bestow with nobility, honour or grace.

ennui.definition: To make bored or listless; to weary.

everlasting.definition: Lasting or enduring forever; existing or continuing without end

evict.definition: To expel (one or more people) from their property; to force (one or more people) to move out.

evince.definition: To show or demonstrate clearly; to manifest.

ewe.definition: A female sheep, as opposed to a ram.

excavate.definition: To make a hole in (something); to hollow.

excerpt.definition: To select or copy sample material (excerpts) from a work.

excoriate.definition: To wear off the skin of; to chafe or flay.

excrete.definition: To discharge material (including waste products) from a cell, body or system.

exculpate.definition: To clear of or to free from guilt; exonerate.

execrate.definition: To feel loathing for; to abhor

exempt.definition: Free from a duty or obligation.
exempt.example: His income is so small that it is exempt from tax.

exhilarate.definition: To cheer, to cheer up, to gladden, to make happy.
exhilarate.example: Good news exhilarates the mind; wine exhilarates the drinker.

exodus.definition: To depart from a place in a large group.

exorbitant.definition: Exceeding proper limits; extravagant; excessive or unduly high.
exorbitant.example: It's a nice car, but they are charging an exorbitant price for it.

expiate.definition: To atone or make reparation for.

exponent.definition: One who expounds, represents or advocates.

expurgate.definition: To edit out (incorrect, offensive, or otherwise undesirable information) from a book or other publication; to cleanse; to purge.
expurgate.example: The publisher decided to expurgate the love scene from the book, to make it more child-friendly.

exquisite.example: Sourav Ganguly scored an exquisite century in his debut Test match.

extemporize.definition: To do something, particularly to perform or speak, without prior planning or thought; to act in an impromptu manner; to improvise.

extenuate.definition: To lessen; to palliate; to lessen or weaken the force of; to diminish the conception of, as crime, guilt, faults, ills, accusations, etc.

extinct.definition: Extinguished, no longer alight (of fire, candles etc.)

extinguish.definition: To put out, as in fire; to end burning; to quench

extraction.definition: An act of extracting or the condition of being extracted.

extraneous.definition: Not belonging to, or dependent upon, a thing; without or beyond a thing; foreign
extraneous.example: Extraneous substances were found on my cup of water.

extravagant.definition: Exceeding the bounds of something; roving; hence, foreign.

extricate.definition: To free, disengage, loosen, or untangle.
extricate.example: I finally managed to extricate myself from the tight jacket.

exuberant.definition: (of people) Very high-spirited; extremely energetic and enthusiastic.

exude.definition: To discharge through pores or incisions, as moisture or other liquid matter; to give out.

exult.definition: To rejoice; to be very happy, especially in triumph.

fable.definition: To compose fables; hence, to write or speak fiction; to write or utter what is not true.

fabricate.definition: To form into a whole by uniting its parts; to construct; to build.

facile.definition: Easy, now especially in a disparaging sense; contemptibly easy.

fallacy.definition: Deceptive or false appearance; that which misleads the eye or the mind.

fallow.definition: (of agricultural land) Ploughed but left unseeded for more than one planting season.

fail-safe.definition: That does not cause undue damage in the event of failure.
fail-safe.example: The system is failsafe, because everything is backed up automatically.

falter.definition: To waver or be unsteady; to weaken or trail off.

fanatic.definition: Showing evidence of possession by a god or demon; frenzied, overzealous.

farce.definition: A style of humor marked by broad improbabilities with little regard to regularity or method.
farce.example: The farce that we saw last night had us laughing and shaking our heads at the same time.

fascinate.definition: To evoke an intense interest or attraction in someone.
fascinate.example: The flickering TV fascinated the cat.

fateful.definition: Momentous, significant, setting or sealing one’s fate.
fateful.example: It started with that fateful trip, history was never the same afterwards.

fathom.definition: To encircle with outstretched arms, especially to take a measurement; to embrace.
fathom.example: I can't for the life of me fathom what this means.

fatigue.definition: To tire or make weary by physical or mental exertion

fatuous.definition: Obnoxiously stupid, vacantly silly, content in one's foolishness.

favorable.example: The candidate wearing the business suite made a favourable impression.

faze.definition: To frighten or cause hesitation; to daunt, put off (usually used in the negative); to disconcert, to perturb.
faze.example: Jumping out of an airplane does not faze him, yet he is afraid to ride a roller coaster.

fecund.definition: Highly fertile; able to produce offspring.

feeble.example: Though she appeared old and feeble, she could still throw a ball.

feign.definition: To make a false show or pretence of; to counterfeit or simulate.
feign.example: The pupil feigned sickness on the day of his exam.

fender.definition: To use fenders to protect the side of a boat

feral.definition: Wild, untamed, especially of domesticated animals having returned to the wild.

fertilize.definition: To make (the soil) more fertile by adding nutrients to it.

fervor.definition: An intense, heated emotion; passion, ardor.
fervor.example: The coach trains his water polo team with fervor.

fester.definition: To become septic; to become rotten.
fester.example: Deal with the problem immediately; do not let it fester.

fetid.example: I caught the fetid odor of dirty socks.

fetter.definition: To shackle or bind up with fetters.

fidelity.example: the fidelity of the civil servants

fidget.definition: To wiggle or twitch; to move around nervously or idly.

figurative.definition: Of use as a metaphor, simile, or metonym, as opposed to literal; using figures; as when saying that someone who eats more than they should is a pig or like a pig.

figurehead.definition: A carved figure on the prow of a sailing ship.

figurine.definition: A small carved or molded figure; a statuette.

filibuster.definition: To take part in a private military action in a foreign country.

filigree.definition: To decorate something with intricate ornamentation made from gold or silver twisted wire.

filing.definition: To commit (official papers) to some office.
filing.example: She filed for divorce the next day.

filly.example: Hey, Homer, get a load of the gams on that filly!

finale.definition: The grand end of something, especially a show or piece of music.

finch.definition: To hunt for finches, to go finching.

finesse.definition: To evade (a problem, situation, etc.) by using some clever argument or strategem.

finicky.definition: (of a person) Fastidious and fussy; difficult to please; exacting, especially about details.

flamboyant.definition: Showy, bold or audacious in behaviour, appearance, etc.

flatter.example: The land around here is flat.

flax.definition: A plant of the genus Linum, especially Linum usitatissimum, which has a single, slender stalk, about a foot and a half high, with blue flowers. Also known as linseed, especially when referring to the seeds.

fledge.definition: Feathered; furnished with feathers or wings; able to fly.

flinch.definition: To make a sudden, involuntary movement in response to a (usually negative) stimulus; to cringe.

flippancy.definition: A disrespectful levity or pertness especially in respect to grave or sacred matters.

flock.definition: To congregate in or head towards a place in large numbers.

florid.definition: Having a rosy or pale red colour; ruddy.

flout.definition: To express contempt for (laws, rules, etc.) by word or action.

fluctuate.example: I fluctuated between wishing he was back home and wishing I'd never met him.

fluke.definition: To obtain a successful outcome by pure chance.

fluster.definition: To make hot and rosy, as with drinking.

fluvial.definition: Of, pertaining to, inhabiting, or produced by the action of a river or stream

foil.definition: To cover or wrap with foil.

foolproof.definition: For a device: protected, or designed to be proof against misuse or error.

footloose.definition: Tending to travel or do as one pleases; readily without many commitments or responsibility.

ford.definition: To cross a stream using a ford.

foreword.definition: An introductory section preceding the main text of a book or other document; a preface or introduction.

forger.definition: A person who creates forgeries, falsifies documents with intent to defraud, e.g. to create a false will or illicit copies of currency; counterfeiter.

formidable.definition: Causing fear, dread, awe, or discouragement as a result of size, strength, or some other impressive feature; commanding respect; causing wonder or astonishment.

fracas.definition: A noisy disorderly quarrel, fight, brawl, disturbance or scrap.

fracture.definition: To break, or cause something to break.

frail.definition: Easily broken physically; not firm or durable; liable to fail and perish

fraudulent.definition: Dishonest; based on fraud or deception.

fraught.definition: The hire of a ship or boat to transport cargo.

fret.definition: Especially when describing animals: to consume, devour, or eat.

friable.definition: Easily broken into small fragments, crumbled, or reduced to powder.

friction.definition: The rubbing of one object or surface against another.

frieze.definition: To make a nap on (cloth); to friz.

frond.definition: The leaf of a fern, especially a compound leaf.

frothy.definition: Foamy or churned to the point of becoming infused with bubbles.

frowsy.definition: Having a dingy, neglected, and scruffy appearance.

full-bodied.definition: Of a comestible, especially a beverage, having a rich flavor.

fumble.example: He fumbled the key into the lock.

fussy.definition: Anxious or particular about petty details.

gadfly.definition: Any dipterous insect of the family Oestridae, commonly known as botflies.
gadfly.example: He's a regular gadfly and takes advantage of his friend's generosity.

gaffe.definition: A foolish and embarrassing error, especially one made in public.

gait.definition: To teach a specific gait to a horse.

galaxy.definition: The Milky Way; the apparent band of concentrated stars which appears in the night sky over earth.

gamble.definition: To take a risk, with the potential of a positive outcome.

gambol.definition: To move about playfully; to frolic.

gangly.definition: Tall and thin, especially so as to cause physical awkwardness.

gangway.definition: To serve as, furnish with, or conduct oneself as though proceeding on a gangway.

gargantuan.definition: Of the giant Gargantua or his appetite.

garish.definition: Overly ostentatious; so colourful as to be in bad taste.

gash.definition: Of poor quality; makeshift; improvised; temporary; substituted.

gaudy.definition: Very showy or ornamented, now especially when excessive, or in a tasteless or vulgar manner

gauge.definition: To measure or determine with a gauge; to measure the capacity of.

gravel.definition: To apply a layer of gravel to the surface of a road, etc.

grieve.definition: To cause sorrow or distress to.

grimace.definition: To make grimaces; to distort one's face; to make faces.

grisly.example: The photographs of the killings depict a grisly scene.

grove.definition: To cultivate in groves; to grow naturally so as to form groves.

grueling.definition: So difficult or taxing as to make one exhausted; backbreaking.

gum.definition: To chew, especially of a toothless person or animal.

gush.definition: To flow forth suddenly, in great volume.

habitable.definition: Safe and comfortable, where humans, or other animals, can live; fit for habitation.

halcyon.definition: Pertaining to the halcyon or kingfisher.

hallmark.definition: To provide or stamp with a hallmark.

hallow.definition: (obsolete outside set phrases) A saint; a holy person; an apostle.

hardy.definition: Having rugged physical strength; inured to fatigue or hardships.

harmonious.definition: Showing accord in feeling or action.

harness.definition: To place a harness on something; to tie up or restrain.

harrow.definition: To drag a harrow over; to break up with a harrow.

hasten.definition: To move or act in a quick fashion.

hatch.definition: To close with a hatch or hatches.

haven.definition: To put into, or provide with a haven.

heinous.example: I hope they catch the person responsible for that heinous crime.

heirloom.definition: A valued possession that has been passed down through the generations.

hem.definition: To make the sound expressed by the word hem; to hesitate in speaking.

hew.definition: To chop away at; to whittle down; to mow down.

herbicide.definition: A substance used to kill plants.

heresy.definition: A doctrine held by a member of a religion at variance with established religious beliefs, especially dissension from Roman Catholic dogma.

heretical.definition: Of or pertaining to heresy or heretics.

hermetic.definition: (chiefly with capital initial) Pertaining to Hermes Trismegistus or the writings attributed to him.

hesitance.definition: The act or state of hesitating.

hie.definition: To hasten; to go quickly, to hurry.

hike.definition: To take a long walk for pleasure or exercise.

histrionic.definition: Of or relating to actors or acting.

hive.definition: To enter or possess a hive.

hoard.definition: To amass, usually for one's own private collection.

hoary.example: The old man bowed his hoary head in acquiescence.

hoax.definition: To deceive (someone) by making them believe something that has been maliciously or mischievously fabricated.

homage.definition: To pay reverence to by external action.

homely.definition: Lacking in beauty or elegance, plain in appearance, physically unattractive.
homely.example: I am seeking a beautiful homely girl for marriage.

homily.definition: A sermon, especially concerning a practical matter.

homogenize.definition: To make homogeneous, to blend or puree.

hone.definition: To sharpen with a hone; to whet.

honorarium.definition: Compensation for services that do not have a predetermined value.

hoodwink.definition: To deceive by disguise; to dupe, bewile, mislead.

horticulture.definition: The art or science of cultivating gardens; gardening.

hovel.definition: To put in a hovel; to shelter.

hub.definition: The central part, usually cylindrical, of a wheel; the nave.

hubris.definition: Excessive pride, presumption or arrogance (originally toward the gods).

humility.definition: The characteristic of being humble; humbleness in character and behavior.

hypnotic.definition: Of, or relating to hypnosis or hypnotism.

hypocritical.definition: Characterized by hypocrisy or being a hypocrite.

idyll.definition: Any poem or short written piece composed in the style of Theocritus' short pastoral poems, the Idylls.

illumination.definition: The act of illuminating, or supplying with light; the state of being illuminated.

illusory.definition: Resulting from an illusion; deceptive, imaginary, unreal

imbibe.definition: To drink (used frequently of alcoholic beverages).

immemorial.example: The rocks had stood overlooking the valley since time immemorial.

immure.definition: To cloister, confine, imprison: to lock up behind walls.
immure.example: John's body was immured Thursday in the mausoleum.

impend.definition: To hang or be suspended over (something); to overhang.

impenetrable.example: The fortress is impenetrable, so it cannot be taken.

impenitent.definition: Not penitent; not repent one's sins

imperative.example: It is imperative that you come here right now.

imperial.definition: Related to an empire, emperor, or empress.

imperturbable.definition: Not easily perturbed, upset or excited.

impervious.definition: Unaffected or unable to be affected by something.

impetuous.definition: Making arbitrary decisions, especially in an impulsive and forceful manner.

implode.definition: To collapse or burst inward violently.

imposing.definition: Magnificent and impressive because of appearance, size, stateliness or dignity.

impostor.definition: Someone who attempts to deceive by using an assumed name or identity.

impotent.definition: Lacking physical strength or vigor; weak

imprecise.definition: Not precise or exact; containing some error or uncertainty

impresario.definition: A manager or producer in the entertainment industry, especially music or theatre.

impromptu.definition: Improvised; without prior preparation, planning or rehearsal.

improvise.example: He had no speech prepared, so he improvised.
synonyms: fly by the seat of one's pants

inalienable.definition: Incapable of being alienated, surrendered, or transferred to another; not alienable.
inalienable.example: inalienable right a right that cannot be given away

inanimate.definition: Lacking the quality or ability of motion; as an inanimate object.

incandescent.definition: Emitting light as a result of being heated

incantation.definition: The act or process of using formulas and/or usually rhyming words, sung or spoken, with occult ceremonies, for the purpose of raising spirits, producing enchantment, or creating other magical results.

incarnate.definition: Embodied in flesh; given a bodily, especially a human, form; personified.

incendiary.definition: Capable of, or used for, or actually causing fire.

inception.definition: The creation or beginning of something; the establishment.

inchoate.definition: Recently started but not fully formed yet; just begun; only elementary or immature.

incinerate.definition: Reduced to ashes by burning; thoroughly consumed.

incipient.definition: In an initial stage; beginning, starting, coming into existence.

incite.definition: To stir up or excite; to rouse or goad into action.

incongruent.definition: Out of place, incompatible, inharmonious, not congruent
incongruent.example: Her knowledge of world politics was incongruent in someone so young.

inconsequential.definition: Having no consequence; not consequential; of little importance.
inconsequential.example: You will never know the exact atomic time when you started reading this phrase; of course, that's inconsequential.

incontrovertible.definition: Not capable of being denied, challenged, or disputed; closed to questioning.
incontrovertible.example: Her statement that Hitler was evil is incontrovertible.

incriminate.definition: To accuse or bring criminal charges against.
incriminate.example: The newspapers incriminated the innocent man unjustly.

incubator.definition: Any apparatus used to maintain environmental conditions suitable for a reaction.

inculpate.definition: To imply the guilt of; to blame or incriminate.

incursion.definition: An aggressive movement into somewhere; an invasion.

indelible.definition: Having the quality of being difficult to delete, remove, wash away, blot out, or efface.
indelible.example: This ink spot on the contract is indelible.

indemnity.definition: Security from damage, loss, or penalty.

indifferent.definition: Not caring or concerned; uninterested, apathetic.
indifferent.example: He was indifferent to the proposal, since it didn’t affect him, either way.

indispensable.definition: Not admitting ecclesiastical dispensation; not subject to release or exemption; that cannot be allowed by bending the canonical rules.
indispensable.example: The law was moral and indispensable. -Bp. Burnet

indoctrinate.definition: To teach with a biased, one-sided or uncritical ideology; to brainwash.

indolent.definition: Habitually lazy, procrastinating, or resistant to physical labor
indolent.example: The indolent girl resisted doing her homework.

inducement.definition: An incentive that helps bring about a desired state. In some contexts, this can imply bribery.

indulgent.definition: Disposed or prone to indulge, humor, gratify, or yield to one's own or another's desires, etc., or to be compliant, lenient, or forbearing;

indurate.example: The doctor removed a lot of indurate skin from his wound.

ineluctable.definition: Impossible to avoid or escape; inescapable, irresistible.

inept.definition: Not able to do something; not proficient; displaying incompetence.

inert.definition: Unable to move or act; inanimate.

inexorable.definition: Impossible to prevent or stop; inevitable.

inferno.definition: A place or situation resembling Hell.

infiltrate.definition: To surreptitiously penetrate, enter or gain access to.
infiltrate.example: High-grade tumors often infiltrate surrounding structures.

infirm.definition: Weak or ill, not in good health.
infirm.example: He was infirm of body but still keen of mind, and though it looked like he couldn't walk across the room, he crushed me in debate.

inflame.definition: To set on fire; to kindle; to cause to burn, flame, or glow.

influx.definition: A flow inward or into something; a coming in.
influx.example: I'll buy a new computer when I get an influx of cash.

infuse.definition: To cause to become an element of something; to insert or fill.
infuse.example: Let it infuse for five minutes.

infuriate.definition: Filled with, characterized by or expressing fury.

ingenious.definition: Displaying genius or brilliance; tending to invent.
ingenious.example: This fellow is ingenious; he fixed a problem I didn't even know I had.

ingest.definition: To take a substance (e.g. food) into the body of an organism, especially through the mouth and into the gastrointestinal tract.

ingrained.definition: Being an element; present in the essence of a thing

ingratiating.definition: Which ingratiates; which attempts to bring oneself into the favour of another, often with flattery or insincerity.

inimitable.definition: Beyond imitation, surpassing all others, matchless.

iniquity.definition: Deviation from what is right; gross injustice, sin, wickedness.

insensible.definition: Unable to be perceived by the senses.

insentient.definition: Having no consciousness or animation; not sentient

instate.definition: To install (someone) in office; to establish.

instigate.definition: To incite; to bring about by urging or encouraging

instrumentalist.definition: One who plays a musical instrument, as distinguished from a vocalist

insular.definition: Of, pertaining to, being, or resembling an island or islands.

insulate.example: Ceramic can be used to insulate power lines.

intangible.definition: Incapable of being perceived by the senses; incorporeal

intelligible.definition: Capable of being understood; clear to the mind.
