interlock.definition: To fit or clasp together securely.

interminable.definition: Existing or occurring without interruption or end; ceaseless, unending.

interregnum.definition: The period of time between the end of a sovereign's reign and the accession of another sovereign.

interrogate.definition: To question or quiz, especially in a thorough and/or aggressive manner
interrogate.example: The police interrogated the suspect at some length before they let him go.

intimidate.definition: To make timid or afraid; to cause to feel fear or nervousness; to deter, especially by threats of violence
intimidate.example: He's trying to intimidate you. If you ignore him, hopefully he'll stop.

intrigue.definition: To conceive or carry out a secret plan intended to harm; to form a plot or scheme.

intrinsic.definition: Innate, inherent, inseparable from the thing itself, essential.
intrinsic.example: the intrinsic merit of an action

intuitive.example: The intuitive response turned out to be correct.

inure.definition: To cause someone to become accustomed to something (usually) unpleasant.
inure.example: Jim buys a beach house that includes the right to travel across the neighbor's property to get to the water. That right of way is said, cryptically, "to inure to the benefit of Jim".

invective.example: Tom's speeches became diatribes — each more invective than the last.

inveigh.definition: (with against or occasionally about, formerly also with on, at, upon) To complain loudly, to give voice to one's censure or criticism

inveigle.definition: To convert, convince, or win over with flattery or wiles.
inveigle.example: He inveigled an introduction to her.

inventory.definition: (operations) To take stock of the resources or items on hand; to produce an inventory.
inventory.example: The main job of the night shift was to inventory the store, and restock when necessary.

invidious.definition: Causing ill will, envy, or offense.

invigorate.definition: To impart vigor, strength, or vitality to.

invincible.definition: Impossible to defeat, destroy or kill; too powerful to be defeated or overcome.

ironclad.definition: Covered with iron, steel, or some metal, armor-plated.
ironclad.example: The suspect had an ironclad alibi for his whereabouts on the night of the crime.

irreducible.definition: Not able to be reduced or lessened.

irrigate.definition: To supply (farmland) with water, by building ditches, pipes, etc.
irrigate.example: We need to irrigate the land before we plant the crops.

irritate.definition: To provoke impatience, anger, or displeasure in.

isthmus.definition: A narrow strip of land, bordered on both sides by water, and connecting two larger landmasses.

itinerary.definition: Itinerant; travelling from place to place; done on a journey

jabber.definition: To talk rapidly, indistinctly, or unintelligibly; to utter gibberish or nonsense.

jaded.definition: Bored or lacking enthusiasm, typically after having been over exposed to, or having consumed too much of something.

jarring.definition: That jars (clashes or disagrees); incongruous, conflictful.

jaundice.definition: To affect with jaundice; to color by prejudice or envy; to prejudice.

jaunty.example: He wore a jaunty outfit that was all the rage.

jest.definition: To tell a joke; to talk in a playful manner; to make fun of something or someone.

jettison.definition: To eject from a boat, submarine, aircraft, spaceship or hot-air balloon, so as to lighten the load.
jettison.example: The ballooners had to jettison all of their sand bags to make it over the final hill.

jibe.definition: To reproach with contemptuous words; to deride, to mock, to taunt.

jocund.definition: Jovial; exuberant; lighthearted; merry and in high spirits; exhibiting happiness.

jog.definition: To push slightly; to move or shake with a push or jerk, as to gain the attention of; to jolt.

jolt.definition: To push or shake abruptly and roughly.
jolt.example: The bus jolted its passengers at every turn.

jot.example: Tell me your order, so I can jot it down.

judicious.definition: Having, characterized by, or done with good judgment or sound thinking.

juggernaut.definition: A literal or metaphorical force or object regarded as unstoppable, that will crush all in its path.

ken.definition: To give birth, conceive, beget, be born; to develop (as a fetus); to nourish, sustain (as life).

kindle.definition: To start (a fire) or light (a torch, a match, coals, etc.).
kindle.example: He kindled an enthusiasm for the project in his fellow workers.

kindred.definition: Of the same nature, or of similar character.

knack.definition: To crack; to make a sharp, abrupt noise; to chink.

knave.definition: A boy; especially, a boy servant.

knead.definition: To work and press into a mass, usually with the hands; especially, to work, as by repeated pressure with the knuckles, into a well mixed mass, the materials of bread, cake, etc.

knit.definition: To turn thread or yarn into a piece of fabric by forming loops that are pulled through each other. This can be done by hand with needles or by machine.
knit.example: The first generation knitted to order; the second still knits for its own use; the next leaves knitting to industrial manufacturers.

kudos.example: The talented, young playwright received much kudos for his new drama.

labile.definition: Liable to slip, err, fall, or apostatize.
labile.example: Certain drugs can be conjugated to polymer molecules with a linkage that is labile at low pH to effect controlled release in a cellular endosome.

labored.example: At the end of the marathon, her laboured breathing told us she was exhausted.

labyrinth.definition: To enclose in a labyrinth, or as though in a labyrinth.

lacerate.definition: Jagged, as if torn or lacerated.
lacerate.example: The bract at the base is dry and papery, often lacerate near its apex.

lackluster.example: The actor gave a lackluster performance in his latest film.

lachrymose.definition: Tearful, sorrowful, sad, pertaining to tears, weeping, causing tears or crying.

lambaste.definition: To scold, reprimand or criticize harshly.
lambaste.example: Her first novel was well and truly lambasted by the critics.

lament.definition: To express grief; to weep or wail; to mourn.

lamentable.definition: Causing sorrow, distress or regret; deplorable, pitiful or distressing.

laminate.definition: Consisting of, or covered with, laminae, or thin plates, scales, or layers, one over another; laminated.

lampoon.definition: To satirize or poke fun at.

landfill.definition: To dispose of (garbage) by burying it at a landfill site.

languish.definition: To lose strength and become weak; to be in a state of weakness or sickness.

languor.definition: A state of the body or mind caused by exhaustion or disease and characterized by a languid or weary feeling; lassitude; an instance of this.

lank.definition: Slender or thin; not well filled out; not plump; shrunken; lean.

lapse.definition: To fall away gradually; to subside.

larder.definition: A cool room in a domestic house where food is stored, but larger than a pantry.

largesse.definition: Generosity in the giving of gifts or money.

lariat.definition: An attack where the wrestler runs towards an opponent, wraps his arm around their upper chest and neck and then forces them to the ground.

lash.definition: To strike with a lash; to whip or scourge with a lash, or with something like one.

lassitude.definition: Lethargy or lack of energy; fatigue.

latent.definition: Existing or present but concealed or inactive.

latitude.definition: The angular distance north or south from a planet's equator, measured along the meridian of that particular point.
latitude.example: His parents gave him a great deal of latitude.

laudatory.definition: Of or pertaining to praise, or the expression of praise.

lavish.definition: Expending or bestowing profusely; profuse; prodigal.
lavish.example: lavish of money;   lavish of praise

leer.definition: To look sideways or obliquely; now especially with sexual desire or malicious intent.

legion.definition: Numerous; vast; very great in number
legion.example: Russia’s labor and capital resources are woefully inadequate to overcome the state’s needs and vulnerabilities, which are legion.

lubricate.definition: To make slippery or smooth (normally to minimize friction) by applying a lubricant.

lucubration.definition: Intense and prolonged study or meditation; especially, late at night.

lug.definition: (sometimes figurative) To haul or drag along (especially something heavy); to carry; to pull.
lug.example: Why do you always lug around so many books?

lugubrious.definition: Gloomy, mournful or dismal, especially to an exaggerated degree.
lugubrious.example: His client’s lugubrious expression tipped off the detective that something lurked beneath her optimistic words.

lull.definition: To cause to rest by soothing influences; to compose; to calm

lumber.definition: To move clumsily and heavily; to move slowly.
lumber.example: I got lumbered with that boring woman all afternoon.

lumen.definition: In the International System of Units, the derived unit of luminous flux; the light that is emitted in a solid angle of one steradian from a source of one candela. Symbol: lm.

luminary.definition: One who is an inspiration to others; one who has achieved success in their chosen field; a leading light.

lurch.definition: To make such a sudden, unsteady movement.

lurk.definition: To remain concealed in order to ambush.

lush.example: That meal was lush! We have to go that restaurant again sometime!

macerate.definition: To soften (something) or separate it into pieces by soaking it in a heated or unheated liquid.

maculate.definition: Marked with spots or maculae; blotched.

malaise.definition: A feeling of general bodily discomfort, fatigue or unpleasantness, often at the onset of illness.

malcontent.definition: Dissatisfied with current conditions; disaffected, discontented, rebellious.

malicious.definition: Of, pertaining to, or as a result of malice or spite
malicious.example: He was sent off for a malicious tackle on Jones.

malign.definition: Evil or malignant in disposition, nature, intent or influence.

malinger.definition: To feign illness, injury, or incapacitation in order to avoid work, obligation, or perilous risk.
malinger.example: It is not uncommon on exam days for several students to malinger rather than prepare themselves.

mangle.definition: To change, mutilate or disfigure by cutting, tearing, rearranging etc.

mangy.example: We stayed in a really mangy hotel in New York.

mania.definition: Violent derangement of mind; madness; insanity.

mannered.definition: (often in combination) Having manners or (often excessive) mannerisms.
mannered.example: His upbringing had led him to act in an overly mannered way.

manumit.definition: To release from slavery, to free.

mar.definition: To spoil; to ruin; to scathe; to damage.

marsh.definition: An area of low, wet land, often with tall grass.

marsupial.definition: Of or pertaining to a marsupial.

marvel.definition: To become filled with wonderment or admiration; to be amazed at something.

masquerade.definition: To take part in a masquerade; to assemble in masks and costumes; to wear a disguise.
masquerade.example: I’m going to masquerade as the wikipede. What are you going to dress up as?

mash.definition: A mass of mixed ingredients reduced to a soft pulpy state by beating or pressure; a mass of anything in a soft pulpy state.

masticate.example: The cow stood, quietly masticating its cud.

mastery.definition: The position or authority of a master; dominion; command; supremacy; superiority.

matriculate.definition: To enroll as a member of a body, especially of a college or university

mattress.definition: To cover with a thick layer, like a mattress; to blanket.

maudlin.definition: Affectionate or sentimental in an effusive, tearful, or foolish manner, especially because of drunkenness.

maven.definition: An expert in a given field.

meager.example: A meager piece of cake in one bite.

measly.definition: Particularly of pigs or pork: infected with larval tapeworms or trichinae (parasitic roundworms).

measured.definition: That has been determined by measurement.
measured.example: He ran over a measured mile.

meddle.definition: To interfere in or with; to concern oneself with unduly.

mediate.definition: Acting through a mediating agency, indirect.

mediocre.definition: Having no peculiar or outstanding features; not extraordinary, special, exceptional, or great; of medium quality, almost always with a negative connotation.
mediocre.example: I'm pretty good at tennis but only mediocre at racquetball.

medley.definition: To combine, to form a medley.

meek.definition: Humble, non-boastful, modest, meager, or self-effacing.

meliorism.definition: The view or doctrine that the world can be improved through human effort (often understood as an intermediate outlook between optimism and pessimism).

melodrama.definition: A kind of drama having a musical accompaniment to intensify the effect of certain scenes.
melodrama.example: the melodrama in the grave digging scene of Beethoven's "Fidelio".

membrane.definition: A flexible enclosing or separating tissue forming a plane or film and separating two environments (usually in a plant or animal).

mendacious.definition: (of a person) Lying, untruthful or dishonest.

mesh.definition: To connect together by interlocking, as gears do.
mesh.example: The music meshed well with the visuals in that film.

mesmerism.definition: The method or power of gaining control over someone's personality or actions, as in hypnosis or suggestion.

metaphysics.definition: The field of study of metaphysics.

metamorphose.definition: (of a moth or insect) To undergo metamorphosis.

meteoric.definition: Of, pertaining to, or originating from a meteor.

meteorology.definition: The science that deals with the study of the atmosphere and its phenomena, especially with weather and weather forecasting.

methodical.definition: In an organized manner; proceeding with regard to method; systematic.
methodical.example: the methodical arrangement of arguments; a methodical treatise

mettle.definition: A quality of endurance and courage.

miff.definition: (usually used in the passive) To offend slightly.

mimicry.definition: The act or ability to simulate the appearance of someone or something else.
mimicry.example: They say that mimicry is the sincerest form of flattery, but I still think I'm being mocked when he acts just like me.

minaret.definition: The tall slender tower of an Islamic mosque, from which the muezzin recites the adhan (call to prayer).

mince.example: Butchers often use machines to mince meat.

mingle.definition: To intermix; to combine or join, as an individual or part, with other parts, but commonly so as to be distinguishable in the product

minutia.definition: A minor detail, often of negligible importance.
minutia.example: They spent all their time on minutiae, never making real progress.

mirage.definition: To cause to appear as or like a mirage.

mire.definition: To cause or permit to become stuck in mud; to plunge or fix in mud.
mire.example: to mire a horse or wagon

mirth.definition: The emotion usually following humour and accompanied by laughter; merriment; jollity; gaiety.

misbehave.definition: To act or behave in an inappropriate, improper, incorrect, or unexpected manner.
misbehave.example: He doesn't mean to misbehave; he just doesn't know better.

mischievous.example: Matthew had a twin brother called Edward, who was always mischievous and badly behaved.

misdemeanor.definition: A crime usually punishable upon conviction by a small fine or by a short term of imprisonment. In the USA, misdemeanants usually are incarcerated in county jail for less than one year, but felons usually are incarcerated in state or federal prison for more than one year. Crimes which are punishable by large fines or by longer imprisonment are sometimes called felonies.

misfortune.definition: An undesirable event such as an accident
misfortune.example: It was my fortune, or misfortune, to be called to the office of Chief Executive without any previous political training. - Ulysses S. Grant

misgiving.definition: Doubt, apprehension, a feeling of dread

mishap.definition: To happen through misfortune; to mishappen.

misrepresent.definition: To represent falsely; to inaccurately portray something.
misrepresent.example: The fraudster misrepresented himself as a lawyer.

mite.definition: Any of many minute arachnids which, along with the ticks, comprise subclass Acarina (aka Acari).

mnemonics.definition: Anything (especially something in verbal form) used to help remember something.
mnemonics.example: To remember the colours of the rainbow, use the mnemonic "Richard of York gave battle in vain" (red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet).

mockery.definition: The action of mocking; ridicule, derision.
mockery.example: The defendant wasn't allowed to speak at his own trial - it was a mockery of justice.

modicum.definition: A modest, small, or trifling amount.
modicum.example: Unable to garner even a modicum of support for his plan, he conceded to follow the others.

molar.definition: Of or relating to the molar teeth, or to grinding.

molding.definition: To shape in or on a mold; to form into a particular shape; to give shape to.
molding.example: These shoes gradually molded to my feet.

mollycoddle.definition: To be overprotective and indulgent toward; to pamper.

molt.definition: To shed or lose a covering of hair or fur, feathers, skin, horns, etc, and replace it with a fresh one.

momentous.definition: Outstanding in importance, of great consequence.

mongrel.definition: Someone or something of mixed kind or uncertain origin, especially a dog.
mongrel.example: That dog is a mongrel; who knows what breed it could be!

monochromatic.definition: Having only one color, represented by differing hues and tints. For example shades in a black and white television.

monocle.definition: A single lens, usually in a wire frame, and used to correct vision for only one eye.

monotonous.definition: Having an unvarying tone or pitch.

montage.definition: To combine into, or depict as, a montage.

moratorium.definition: An authorization to a debtor, permitting temporary suspension of payments.
moratorium.example: Canada may put a moratorium on cloning for research.

mordant.definition: Having or showing a sharp or critical quality

moribund.definition: Approaching death; about to die; dying; expiring.

mortar.definition: To use mortar or plaster to join two things together.

mosaic.definition: (of an individual) Containing cells of varying genetic constitution.

moth-eaten.definition: Containing holes by having been eaten by moth larvae.

motile.definition: Having the power to move spontaneously.

motley.definition: Comprising greatly varied elements, to the point of incongruity; heterogeneous.

mottle.definition: To mark with blotches of different color, or shades of color, as if stained; to spot; to maculate.

motto.definition: A sentence, phrase, or word, forming part of an heraldic achievement.

mournful.definition: Filled with grief or sadness; being in a state in which one mourns.

muddy.definition: Covered with or full of mud or wet soil.
muddy.example: He slogged across the muddy field.

muffler.definition: Part of the exhaust pipe of a car that dampens the noise the engine produces.

mulish.definition: Characteristic of a mule; stubborn, obstinate, or intractable

mumble.definition: To speak unintelligibly or inaudibly; to fail to articulate.
mumble.example: Please try not to mumble so I can hear you better.

mural.definition: Of or relating to a wall; on, or in, or against a wall.

murky.definition: Hard to see through, as a fog or mist.

murmur.definition: To grumble; to complain in a low, muttering voice, or express discontent at or against someone or something.
murmur.example: I couldn't hear the words; he just murmured a lot.

musket.definition: A kind of firearm formerly carried by the infantry of an army, originally fired by means of a match, or matchlock, for which several mechanical appliances (including the flintlock, and finally the percussion lock) were successively substituted; ultimately superseded by the rifle.
musket.example: Sam, Sam, pick up thy musket.

mute.definition: Not having the power of speech; dumb.

mutter.definition: To utter words, especially complaints or angry expressions, indistinctly or with a low voice and lips partly closed; to say under one's breath.
mutter.example: The beggar muttered words of thanks, as passersby dropped coins in his cup.

nadir.definition: To extend (a beehive) by adding an empty box at the base.

naive.definition: Lacking worldly experience, wisdom, or judgement; unsophisticated.

nautical.definition: Relating to or involving ships or shipping or navigation or seamen.

nebulous.definition: In the form of a cloud or haze; hazy.

needy.example: Needy people want to give too, but have few material goods to offer.

nefarious.definition: Sinful, villainous, criminal, or wicked, especially when noteworthy or notorious for such characteristics.
nefarious.example: Aliens have a nefarious connotation in many science fiction books.

neologism.definition: A word or phrase which has recently been coined; a new word or phrase.

neophyte.definition: A beginner; a person who is new to a subject, skill, or belief.

nepotism.definition: The favoring of relatives or personal friends because of their relationship rather than because of their abilities.
nepotism.example: Nepotism can get you very far in the world if you've got the right connections.

nettle.definition: Of the nettle plant and similar physical causes, to sting, causing a rash in someone.
nettle.example: The children were badly nettled after playing in the field.

neutralize.definition: To make even, inactive or ineffective.

nicety.example: We met the new captain while we were taking enemy fire and were unable to observe the niceties of formal introductions.

nibble.definition: To eat with small, quick bites.
nibble.example: The rabbit nibbled at the lettuce.

nip.definition: A small quantity of something edible or a potable liquor.
nip.example: He had a nip of whiskey.

nitpick.definition: To correct minutiae or find fault in unimportant details.
nitpick.example: Someone will invariably nitpick about any spelling error posted to the forums.

nocturnal.definition: (of a person, creature, group, or species) Primarily active during the night.

nomad.definition: Of or relating to nomads, whether

nominal.definition: Of, resembling, relating to, or consisting of a name or names.

nonchalant.example: We handled the whole frenetic situation with a nonchalant attitude.

nondescript.example: He drove a nondescript silver sedan.

nonplus.definition: To perplex or bewilder someone; to confound or flummox

notch.example: The tribe's hunters notch their kills by notches on each's axe's handle.

nudge.definition: To push against gently, especially in order to gain attention or give a signal.

nugatory.definition: Trivial, trifling or of little importance.

oasis.definition: A spring of fresh water, surrounded by a fertile region of vegetation, in a desert.
oasis.example: The park was an oasis in the middle of the busy city.

oath.definition: A solemn pledge or promise, appealing to a deity, a ruler, or another entity (not necessarily present) to attest to the truth of a statement or sincerity of one's desire to fulfill a contract or promise.
oath.example: After taking the oath of office, she became the country's forty-third premier.

obedient.definition: Willing to comply with the commands, orders, or instructions of those in authority.
obedient.example: Jessica was so intensely obedient of her parents that her brother sometimes thought she was a robot.

obeisance.definition: Demonstration of an obedient attitude, especially by bowing deeply; a deep bow which demonstrates such an attitude.

obituary.definition: Relating to the death of a person.

obligatory.definition: Imposing obligation, legally, morally, or otherwise; binding; mandatory.

oblique.definition: Not erect or perpendicular; neither parallel to, nor at right angles from, the base; slanting; inclined.

obliterate.definition: To remove completely, leaving no trace; to wipe out; to destroy.

oblivious.definition: (usually followed by to or of) Lacking awareness; unmindful; unaware, unconscious of.

observatory.definition: A place where stars, planets and other celestial bodies are observed, usually through a telescope; also place for observing meteorological or other natural phenomena.

obstreperous.definition: Attended by, or making, a loud and tumultuous noise; boisterous.

obstruct.definition: To block or fill (a passage) with obstacles or an obstacle.

obtuse.definition: Blunt; not sharp, pointed, or acute in form.

obviate.definition: To anticipate and prevent or bypass (something which would otherwise have been necessary or required).

occult.definition: Secret; hidden from general knowledge; undetected.

ode.definition: A short poetical composition proper to be set to music or sung; a lyric poem; especially, now, a poem characterized by sustained noble sentiment and appropriate dignity of style.

odium.example: His conduct brought him into odium, or, brought odium upon him.

offbeat.example: He has such an offbeat sense of humor that hardly anyone finds his jokes amusing.

off-key.definition: Pitched inaccurately either above or below the correct note

olfaction.definition: The sense of smell; the detection of airborne molecules.

ominous.definition: Of or pertaining to an omen or to omens; being or exhibiting an omen; significant.
ominous.example: California poll support for Jerry Brown's tax increases has ominous implications for U.S. taxpayers too Los Angeles Times Headline April 25, 2011

onerous.definition: Imposing or constituting a physical, mental, or figurative load which can be borne only with effort; burdensome.

opine.definition: To have or express an opinion; to state as an opinion; to suppose, consider (that).
opine.example: "Your decisions," she opined, "have been unfailingly disastrous for this company."

opportune.example: This would be an opportune spot for a picnic.

opprobrious.definition: Of or relating to opprobrium or disgrace.

oration.definition: To deliver an oration; to speak.

ornithology.definition: The branch of zoology that deals with the scientific study of birds.

orthodox.definition: Conforming to the accepted, established, or traditional doctrines of a given faith, religion, or ideology.

oscillate.definition: To swing back and forth, especially if with a regular rhythm.
oscillate.example: A pendulum oscillates slower as it gets longer.

ossify.definition: To transform (or cause to transform) from a softer animal substance into bone; particularly the processes of growth in humans and animals.

oust.example: The protesters became so noisy that they were finally ousted from the meeting.

outgoing.definition: Extraverted: talkative, friendly, and social, especially with respect to meeting new people easily and comfortably.
outgoing.example: Tom is very outgoing and enjoys meeting people; his brother, on the other hand, is painfully shy.

outlandish.example: The rock star wore black with outlandish pink and green spiked hair.

outmaneuver.definition: To perform movements more adroitly or successfully than.

outset.definition: The beginning or initial stage of something.
outset.example: He agreed and understood from the outset, so don't bother explaining again.

outwit.definition: To get the better of; to outsmart, to beat in a competition of wits.

overlap.definition: To extend over and partly cover something.
overlap.example: The mouse-mat overlapped the edge of the desk.

overt.definition: Open and not concealed or secret.

overture.definition: To make overtures; to approach with a proposal.

oxymoron.definition: A figure of speech in which two words or phrases with opposing meanings are used together intentionally for effect.

pachyderm.definition: A member of the obsolete taxonomic order Pachydermata, grouping of thick-skinned, hoofed animals such as the rhinoceros, hippopotamus, elephant, pig and horse.

pacify.definition: To bring peace to (a place or situation), by ending war, fighting, violence, anger or agitation.

padding.definition: To furnish with a pad or padding.

paean.definition: To sing a paean; to praise.

painstaking.definition: Carefully attentive to details; diligent in performing a process or procedure.

palatable.example: For some instant noodles make a palatable, if not especially nutritious, meal.

palatial.definition: Of or relating to a palace.
palatial.example: The home where he lived was palatial.

palette.definition: A thin board on which a painter lays and mixes colours.

palmy.definition: Made out of palm leaves or palm sap.

palpable.definition: Capable of being touched, felt or handled; touchable, tangible.

palter.definition: To talk insincerely; to prevaricate or equivocate in speech or actions.

paltry.example: She made some paltry excuse and left.

pancreas.definition: A gland near the stomach which secretes a fluid into the duodenum to help with food digestion. The fluid contains protease, carbohydrase and lipase, which breaks down larger molecules into smaller pieces. The pancreas also produces the hormones insulin and glucagon which regulate blood sugar. These hormones are released into the cardiovascular system.

pandemic.definition: Of a disease: epidemic over a wide geographical area and affecting a large proportion of the population; also, of or pertaining to a disease of this nature.
pandemic.example: World War I might have continued indefinitely if not for a pandemic outbreak of influenza.

pandemonium.definition: A place where all demons live; Hell.

pantry.definition: A small room, closet, or cabinet usually located in or near the kitchen, dedicated to shelf-stable food storage and/or storing kitchenware, like a larder, but smaller.

paradigm.definition: A pattern, a way of doing something, especially a pattern of thought, a system of beliefs, a conceptual framework.
paradigm.example: Thomas Kuhn's landmark “The Structure of Scientific Revolutions” got people talking about paradigm shifts, to the point the word itself now suggests an incomplete or biased perspective.

paradox.definition: An apparently self-contradictory statement, which can only be true if it is false, and vice versa.
paradox.example: "This sentence is false" is a paradox.

paragon.definition: To compare; to parallel; to put in rivalry or emulation with.

paramount.example: Getting those credit cards paid off is paramount.

paranoia.definition: A psychotic disorder characterized by delusions of persecution, conspiracy and perceived threat against the person, often associated with false accusations and general mistrust of others

paraphrase.definition: To restate something as, or to compose a paraphrase.

parasite.definition: A person who lives on other people's efforts or expense and gives little or nothing back.
parasite.example: Lice, fleas, ticks and mites are widely spread parasites.

parch.definition: To burn the surface of, to scorch.
parch.example: The sun today could parch cement.

parenthesis.definition: A clause, phrase or word which is inserted (usually for explanation or amplification) into a passage which is already grammatically complete, and usually marked off with brackets, commas or dashes.

pariah.definition: A person who is rejected from society or home; an outcast.

parity.definition: Equality; comparability of strength or intensity.

parlance.definition: A certain way of speaking, of using words, especially when it comes to those with a particular job or interest.

parody.definition: To make a parody of something.
parody.example: The comedy movie parodied the entire Western genre.

paroxysm.definition: A random or sudden outburst (of activity).

parquetry.definition: The technique of applying wooden tiles or veneers to create a decorative geometrical pattern on floors, furniture etc.
parquetry.example: The new ballroom floor is a marvellous example of parquetry.

parrot.definition: To repeat (exactly what has just been said) without necessarily showing understanding, in the manner of a parrot.
parrot.example: The interviewee merely parroted the views of her tabloid.

parry.definition: To avoid, deflect, or ward off (an attack, a blow, an argument, etc.).

parse.definition: To resolve (a sentence, etc.) into its elements, pointing out the several parts of speech, and their relation to each other by agreement or government; to analyze and describe grammatically.

parsimony.definition: Great reluctance to spend money unnecessarily.

particularize.definition: To make particular, as opposed to general; to restrict to a specific or individual case, class etc.; to single out.

partisan.definition: Serving as commander or member of a body of detached light troops.

pastiche.definition: To create or compose in a mixture of styles.

partition.definition: To divide something into parts, sections or shares

pathology.definition: The branch of medicine concerned with the study of the nature of disease and its causes, processes, development, and consequences.
pathology.example: The surgeon sent a specimen of the cyst to the pathology department for staining and analysis to determine its histologic subtype.

patina.definition: Of a green colour, tinted with grey, like that of bronze patina.

patrician.definition: Of or pertaining to the Roman patres ("fathers") or senators, or patricians.

patriot.definition: A person who loves and zealously supports and defends their country.

paunchy.definition: Having a paunch; having a prominent stomach; potbellied.
paunchy.example: He exercised every day to avoid becoming paunchy in his old age.

pebble.example: to pebble the ice between games

peck.definition: To strike or pierce with the beak or bill (of a bird).
peck.example: The birds pecked at their food.

pedagogue.definition: A teacher or instructor of children; one whose occupation is to teach the young.

pedestal.definition: To set or support on (or as if on) a pedestal.

predestine.definition: To determine the future or the fate of something in advance; to preordain.

pediatrics.definition: The branch of medicine that deals with the treatment of children.

peep.definition: To make a soft, shrill noise like a baby bird.

peeve.example: He was peeved to note that his hard work had been undone.

pelf.definition: Money; riches; gain, especially when dishonestly acquired (compare lucre).

pellucid.definition: Allowing the passage of light; transparent.

penchant.example: He has a penchant for fine wine.

penitent.definition: Feeling pain or sorrow on account of one's sins or offenses; feeling sincere guilt.

pensive.definition: Having the appearance of deep, often melancholic, thinking.

perambulate.definition: To walk about, roam or stroll.

perceptible.definition: Able to be perceived, sensed, or discerned.
perceptible.example: Her voice was barely perceptible over the noise, but her gestures made her meaning clear.

peremptory.definition: Precluding debate or expostulation; not admitting of question or appeal

perfervid.definition: Extremely, excessively, or feverishly passionate; zealous.

perforate.example: to perforate a sheet of postage stamps

perfunctory.definition: Done only to fulfil a duty, or in a careless or indifferent manner; performed mechanically and as a thing of rote.
perfunctory.example: He did a perfunctory job cleaning his dad's car, finishing quickly but leaving a few spots still dirty.

perimeter.definition: The sum of the distance of all the lengths of the sides of an object.

periodical.definition: Published at regular intervals of more than one day, especially weekly, monthly, or quarterly.

perish.definition: To decay and disappear; to waste away to nothing.

perjury.definition: The deliberate giving of false or misleading testimony under oath.
perjury.example: We declare under penalty of perjury that the foregoing is true and correct.

permeable.definition: That absorbs or allows the passage of fluids
permeable.example: Rainwater sinks through permeable rock to form an underground reservoir.

peroration.definition: The concluding section of a discourse, either written or oral, in which the orator or writer sums up and commends his topic to his audience, particularly as used in the technical sense of a component of ancient Roman oratorical delivery.

perpetual.definition: Lasting forever, or for an indefinitely long time
perpetual.example: By means of artificial hybridization, practised for a series of years, he has succeeded in producing a race of carnations which are perpetual bloomers.

persecute.definition: To pursue in a manner to injure, grieve, or afflict; to beset with cruelty or malignity; to harass; especially, to afflict, harass, punish, or put to death for one's race, sexual identity, adherence to a particular religious creed, or mode of worship.
persecute.example: He who persecutes one will persecute all.

persevere.definition: To persist steadfastly in pursuit of an undertaking, task, journey, or goal, even if hindered by distraction, difficulty, obstacles, or discouragement.

persistence.example: You've got to admire his persistence. He's asked her out every day for a month even though she keeps turning him down.

personable.definition: (of a person) Having a pleasing appearance or manner; attractive; handsome; friendly; amiable.

plasticity.definition: The quality or state of being plastic.

plateau.definition: To reach a stable level; to level off.

plausible.definition: Seemingly or apparently valid, likely, or acceptable; conceivably true or likely

pleat.definition: To form one or more pleats in a piece of fabric or a garment.

plentitude.definition: Abundance, fullness, completeness; an instance of this.
plentitude.example: Of quotations in the OED there are indeed a plentitude.

plethora.definition: (usually followed by of) An excessive amount or number; an abundance.
plethora.example: The menu offers a plethora of cuisines from around the world.

pliable.definition: Soft, flexible, easily bent, formed, shaped, or molded.
pliable.example: You will find the clay perfectly pliable as long as it stays moist.

pliant.definition: Capable of plying or bending; readily yielding to force or pressure without breaking

pluck.definition: To pull something sharply; to pull something out
pluck.example: She plucked the phone from her bag and dialled.

plumb.definition: Truly vertical, as indicated by a plumb line.

plummet.definition: To drop swiftly, in a direct manner; to fall quickly.
plummet.example: After its ascent, the arrow plummeted to earth.

plump.definition: Having a full and rounded shape; chubby, somewhat overweight.

podiatrist.definition: A health care practitioner who specializes in the diagnosis and treatment of foot ailments.
podiatrist.example: I visited my podiatrist to get a new pair of orthopedic inserts for my sneakers.

polarize.definition: To cause to have a polarization.

polished.definition: Made smooth or shiny by polishing.

politic.definition: Of or relating to polity, or civil government; political.

pollster.definition: A professional who conducts or analyzes opinion polls.
pollster.example: Trump didn't like the results of the polls, so he fired the pollsters.

ponderable.definition: Having a detectable amount of matter; having a measurable mass.

pontificate.definition: The status or term of office of a pontiff or pontifex.

porcelain.definition: The plant Smilax china, a liana of much of eastern Asia.

pore.definition: A tiny opening in the skin.

porous.definition: Full of tiny pores that allow fluids or gasses to pass through.
porous.example: Concrete is porous, so water will slowly filter through it.

poseur.definition: One who affects some behaviour, style, attitude or other condition, often to impress or influence others.
poseur.example: He pretends he's an artist, but he's just a poseur.

posture.definition: To put one's body into a posture or series of postures, especially hoping that one will be noticed and admired
posture.example: If you're finished posturing in front of the mirror, can I use the bathroom now?

potable.definition: Good for drinking without fear of disease or poisoning.

potboiler.definition: (mildly) A creative work of low quality (book, art, etc), produced merely to earn a living or for profit, as opposed to serious creative expression.

potentiate.example: These antidepressants potentiate the analgesic action of opiates.

pout.example: "Don't you love me any more?" she pouted.

prairie.definition: An extensive area of relatively flat grassland with few, if any, trees, especially in North America.

prate.definition: To talk much and to little purpose; to be loquacious; to speak foolishly.

precipitate.example: The king was too precipitate in declaring war.

precipitation.definition: Any or all of the forms of water particles, whether liquid or solid, that fall from the atmosphere (e.g., rain, hail, snow or sleet). It is a major class of hydrometeor, but it is distinguished from cloud, fog, dew, rime, frost, etc., in that it must fall. It is distinguished from cloud and virga in that it must reach the ground.

precursor.definition: (of intersymbol interference) Caused by the following symbol.

preeminent.definition: Exceeding others in quality or rank; of outstanding excellence, extremely notable or important.

preface.definition: To introduce or make a comment before (the main point).
preface.example: Let me preface this by saying that I don't know him that well.

premeditate.definition: To meditate, consider, or plan beforehand; to think about and revolve in the mind beforehand.

preoccupation.definition: The state of being preoccupied or an idea that preoccupies the mind; enthrallment.

preponderant.definition: Having greater or the greatest weight, quantity, importance or force.
preponderant.example: The judge gave preponderant weight to the physical evidence.

prepossessing.definition: Tending to invite favor; attracting confidence, favor, esteem, or love; attractive

preposterous.definition: Absurd, or contrary to common sense.

preservative.example: The curators took preservative actions to maintain the ancient collection.

prestige.definition: (of a linguistic form) Regarded as relatively prestigious; often, considered the standard language or language variety, or a part of such a variety.

preternatural.definition: Beyond or not conforming to what is natural or according to the regular course of things; strange.

primordial.example: a primordial leaf; a primordial cell

primp.definition: To spend time improving one's appearance, often in front of a mirror.

privation.definition: The state of being deprived of or lacking an attribute formerly or properly possessed; the loss or absence of such an attribute.

probity.definition: Integrity, especially of the quality of having strong moral principles; honesty and decency.

proclivity.definition: A predisposition or natural inclination, propensity, or a predilection; especially, a strong disposition or bent.
proclivity.example: The child has a proclivity for exaggeration.

procrastinate.definition: To delay taking action; to wait until later.
procrastinate.example: He procrastinated until the last minute and had to stay up all night to finish.

prod.definition: To poke, to push, to touch.

profane.definition: Unclean; ritually impure; unholy, desecrating a holy place or thing.

proffer.definition: To offer for acceptance; to propose to give; to make a tender of.
proffer.example: to proffer friendship, a gift, or services

proficient.definition: Good at something; skilled; fluent; practiced, especially in relation to a task or skill.
proficient.example: He was a proficient writer with an interest in human nature.

profundity.definition: The state of being profound or abstruse.

prohibitive.definition: Tending to prohibit, preclude, or disallow.
prohibitive.example: Some countries are more prohibitive than others when it comes to hot topics like euthanasia and cloning.

proliferate.definition: To increase in number or spread rapidly; to multiply.
proliferate.example: The flowers proliferated rapidly all spring.

prolix.definition: Tediously lengthy; dwelling on trivial details.

prologue.definition: To introduce with a formal preface, or prologue.

prolong.definition: To extend in space or length.
prolong.example: The government shouldn't prolong deciding on this issue any further.

proofread.definition: To check a written text for errors in spelling and grammar.

propagate.definition: (of animals or plants) To cause to continue or multiply by generation, or successive production

propensity.definition: An inclination, disposition, tendency, preference, or attraction.
propensity.example: He has a propensity for lengthy discussions of certain favorite topics.

prophetic.definition: Of, or relating to a prophecy or a prophet

propitiate.definition: To conciliate, appease, or make peace with someone, particularly a god or spirit.

propriety.definition: The particular character or essence of someone or something; individuality.

prosaic.definition: Pertaining to or having the characteristics of prose.
prosaic.example: The tenor of Eliot's prosaic work differs greatly from that of his poetry.

proscribe.example: The law proscribes driving a car while intoxicated.

proselytize.definition: To advertise one’s religious beliefs; to convert (someone) to one’s own faith or religious movement or encourage them to do so.
proselytize.example: It is illegal to proselytize [children] in some countries

prosperous.example: Trading Babe Ruth was far more prosperous for the Yankees than for the Red Sox.

prostrate.example: He was prostrate from the extreme heat.

protract.definition: To draw out; to extend, especially in duration.
protract.example: to protract a decision or duty

protrude.definition: To extend from, above or beyond a surface or boundary; to bulge outward; to stick out.

provident.definition: Possessing, exercising, or demonstrating great care and consideration for the future.

provisory.example: The provisory clause effectively excluded his otherwise logical heirs from his main inheritance.

prowess.definition: Skillfulness and manual ability; adroitness or dexterity.

prowl.definition: To rove over, through, or about in a stealthy manner; especially, to search in, as for prey or booty.
prowl.example: It's tough to sneak vandalism into Wikipedia as there are plenty of other users prowling the Recent Changes page.

prude.definition: A person who is or tries to be excessively proper, especially one who is easily offended by matters of a sexual nature.

prudent.definition: Sagacious in adapting means to ends; circumspect in action, or in determining any line of conduct; careful, discreet, sensible; — opposed to rash; directed by prudence or wise forethought; evincing prudence
prudent.example: His prudent career moves reliably brought him to the top

prudish.definition: Of excessive propriety; easily offended or shocked, especially by sexual matters

prune.definition: To become wrinkled like a dried plum, as the fingers and toes do when kept submerged in water.

pry.definition: To look where one is not welcome; to be nosy.

pseudonym.definition: A fictitious name, as those used by writers and movie stars.
pseudonym.example: The Reverend Charles Lutwidge Dodgson wrote "Alice's Adventures in Wonderland" under the pseudonym Lewis Carroll.

pucker.definition: To pinch or wrinkle; to squeeze inwardly, to dimple or fold.

pulverize.definition: To render into dust or powder.

pun.definition: To beat; strike with force; to ram; to pound, as in a mortar; reduce to powder, to pulverize.
pun.example: We punned about the topic until all around us groaned.

punctilious.definition: Strictly attentive to detail; meticulous or fastidious, particularly to codes or conventions.
punctilious.example: With a punctilious slap of the gloves, the duel was now inevitable.

pungent.definition: Having a strong odor that stings the nose, said especially of acidic or spicy substances.
pungent.example: I accidentally dropped the bottle of ammonia and after few seconds, a very pungent stench could be detected.

puny.definition: Of inferior size, strength or significance; small, weak, ineffective.
puny.example: You puny earthlings are no match for Ming the Merciless!

purity.definition: The state or degree of being pure.

purlieu.definition: The ground on the edges of a forest, especially when partly subject to the same forest laws concerning game hunting etc.

purloin.definition: To take the property of another, often in breach of trust; to appropriate wrongfully; to steal.

purvey.definition: To prepare in advance (for or to do something); to plan, make provision.

pusillanimous.definition: Showing ignoble cowardice, or contemptible timidity.

quack.definition: To make a noise like a duck.
quack.example: Do you hear the ducks quack?

quaff.definition: To drink or imbibe with vigour or relish; to drink copiously; to swallow in large draughts.

quail.definition: To waste away; to fade, to wither

quarantine.definition: The desert in which Christ fasted for 40 days according to the Bible.

quarry.definition: To obtain (or mine) stone by extraction from a quarry.
quarry.example: They quarried out new, interesting facts about ancient Egypt from old papyri.

quaver.definition: To shake in a trembling manner.

quell.definition: To subdue, to put down; to silence or force (someone) to submit.

quench.definition: To satisfy, especially an actual or figurative thirst.
quench.example: The library quenched her thirst for knowledge.

quibble.definition: To complain or argue in a trivial or petty manner.
quibble.example: They are constantly quibbling over insignificant details.

quiescent.example: The bats were quiescent at that time of day, so we slowly entered the cave.

quixotic.definition: Possessing or acting with the desire to do noble and romantic deeds, without thought of realism and practicality; exceedingly idealistic.

rabble.definition: To speak in a confused manner; talk incoherently; utter nonsense

raconteur.definition: To make witty remarks or stories.

reap.definition: To cut (for example a grain) with a sickle, scythe, or reaping machine
reap.example: to reap a benefit from exertions

rebuff.definition: To refuse; to offer sudden or harsh resistance; to turn down or shut out.

recalcitrant.definition: Marked by a stubborn unwillingness to obey authority.

recant.definition: To withdraw or repudiate a statement or opinion formerly expressed, especially formally and publicly.
recant.example: Convince me that I am wrong, and I will recant.

recidivism.definition: Committing new offenses after a crime committed in the past.

reciprocate.definition: To exchange two things, with both parties giving one thing and taking another thing.
reciprocate.example: I gave them apples from my tree; they reciprocated with a pie and some apple jelly.

reckless.definition: Careless or heedless; headstrong or rash.

recoil.definition: To pull back, especially in disgust, horror or astonishment.
recoil.example: He recoiled in disgust when he saw the mess.

reconnoiter.definition: To perform a reconnaissance (of an area; an enemy position); to scout with the aim of acquiring information.
reconnoiter.example: Our scout will reconnoiter the path ahead of our troops.

reconstitute.definition: To construct something anew, or in a different manner

reconvene.definition: To resume something that has been convened and then paused.
reconvene.example: We will reconvene after lunch to discuss the outstanding points on the agenda.

rectitude.definition: Straightness; the state or quality of having a constant direction and not being crooked or bent.

recuperate.definition: To recover, especially from an illness; to get better from an illness.

redistribution.definition: The act of changing the distribution of resources.
redistribution.example: The redistribution of wealth between the rich and poor has been tried many times.

redolent.definition: Fragrant or aromatic; having a sweet scent.

redoubtable.definition: Eliciting respect or fear; imposing; awe-inspiring.
redoubtable.example: The redoubtable New York Times has been called the "newspaper of record" of the United States.

reel.example: He reeled off some tape from the roll and sealed the package.

refine.definition: To purify; reduce to a fine, unmixed, or pure state; to free from impurities.

refulgent.definition: Resplendent, or shining brightly and radiantly.

regimen.definition: Orderly government; system of order; administration.

regress.definition: To move backwards to an earlier stage; to devolve.
regress.example: When we regress Y on X, we use the values of variable X to predict those of Y.

rehabilitate.definition: To restore (someone) to their former state, reputation, possessions, status etc.

rehearsal.definition: The practicing of something which is to be performed before an audience, usually to test or improve the interaction between several participating people, or to allow technical adjustments with respect to staging to be done.

rein.definition: To direct or stop a horse by using reins.

rejoice.definition: To be very happy, be delighted, exult; to feel joy.

relapse.definition: To fall back again; to slide or turn back into a former state or practice.
relapse.example: He has improved recently but keeps relapsing into states of utter confusion.

relinquish.definition: To give up, abandon or retire from something. To trade away.

relish.definition: To taste or eat with pleasure, to like the flavor of

remodel.definition: To change the appearance, layout, or furnishings of.

remonstrance.definition: A remonstration; disapproval; a formal, usually written, objection or protest.

remorse.definition: A feeling of regret or sadness for doing wrong or sinning.

remunerate.example: The workers were generously remunerated for their time.

rend.definition: To separate into parts with force or sudden violence; to split; to burst

renegade.definition: To desert one's cause, or change one's loyalties; to commit betrayal.

renounce.definition: To give up, resign, surrender, atsake.
renounce.example: to renounce a title to land or to a throne

renovate.definition: To renew; to revamp something to make it look new again.

repatriate.definition: To restore (a person) to his or her own country.

repel.definition: To turn (someone) away from a privilege, right, job, etc.

repertoire.definition: A list of dramas, operas, pieces, parts, etc., which a company or a person has rehearsed and is prepared to perform or display.
repertoire.example: The conjurer expanded his repertoire with some new tricks.

retinue.definition: A group of servants or attendants, especially of someone considered important.

retort.definition: To say something sharp or witty in answer to a remark or accusation.
retort.example: to retort the charge of vanity

retouch.definition: To improve something (especially a photograph), by adding or correcting details, or by removing flaws.

retrench.definition: To dig or redig a trench where one already exists.

retribution.definition: Punishment inflicted in the spirit of moral outrage or personal vengeance.

retrograde.definition: Directed backwards, retreating; reverting, especially to an inferior state, declining; inverse, reverse; movement opposite to normal or intended motion, often circular motion.

retrospective.definition: Of, relating to, or contemplating the past.

revere.definition: To regard someone or something with great awe or devotion.

revoke.definition: To cancel or invalidate by withdrawing or reversing.
revoke.example: I hereby revoke all former wills.

revolt.example: The farmers had to revolt against the government to get what they deserved.

ribald.definition: Coarsely, vulgarly, or lewdly amusing; referring to sexual matters in a rude or irreverent way.

rickety.definition: Of an object: not strong or sturdy, as because of poor construction or upkeep; not safe or secure.
rickety.example: He hesitated about climbing such a small, rickety ladder.

rider.definition: One who rides, often on a horse or a motorcycle.

ridicule.definition: To criticize or disapprove of someone or something through scornful jocularity; to make fun of
ridicule.example: His older sibling constantly ridiculed him with sarcastic remarks.

rife.definition: Widespread, common, prevalent, current (mainly of unpleasant or harmful things).
rife.example: Smallpox was rife after the siege had been lifted.

rift.definition: To form a rift; to split open.

ripen.definition: To grow ripe; to become mature (said of grain, fruit, flowers etc.)

rive.definition: To tear apart by force; to rend; to split; to cleave.

rivulet.definition: A small brook or stream; a streamlet.
rivulet.example: A rivulet of tears ran down his face.

rodent.definition: Gnawing; biting; corroding; applied to a destructive variety of cancer or ulcer.

roil.definition: To render turbid by stirring up the dregs or sediment of.

roister.definition: To engage in noisy, drunken, or riotous behavior.

rookie.example: The game was going well until I made that rookie mistake.

roster.definition: To place the name of (a person) on a roster.
roster.example: I have rostered you for cleaning duties on the first Monday of each month.

rostrum.definition: A dais, pulpit, or similar platform for a speaker, conductor, or other performer.

rouse.definition: To wake (someone) or be awoken from sleep, or from apathy.
rouse.example: to rouse the faculties, passions, or emotions

royalty.definition: The rank, status, power or authority of a monarch.

ruffle.definition: To make a ruffle in; to curl or flute, as an edge of fabric.
ruffle.example: Ruffle the end of the cuff.

ruminate.definition: Having a hard albumen penetrated by irregular channels filled with softer matter, as the nutmeg and the seeds of the North American papaw.

rumple.example: I'll rumple my bedsheets so it looks like I was here last night.

rupture.definition: To burst, break through, or split, as under pressure.

ruse.definition: To deceive or trick using a ruse.

rustle.definition: To move (something) with a soft crackling sound.

savory.example: The fine restaurant presented an array of savory dishes; each was delicious.

scabbard.definition: To put an object (especially a sword) into its scabbard.

scads.definition: Any of several fish, of the family Carangidae, from the western Atlantic.

scalpel.definition: A small straight knife with a very sharp blade used for surgery, dissection and craftwork.

scant.example: After his previous escapades, Mary had scant reason to believe John.

schism.definition: A split or separation within a group or organization, typically caused by discord.
schism.example: The schism between Sunnis and Shias happened quite early in Islamic history.

scintillate.definition: To give off sparks; to shine as if emanating sparks; to twinkle or glow.

scion.definition: A descendant, especially a first-generation descendant of a distinguished family.

scission.definition: The act of division, separation, cutting or severing

scissor.definition: To cut using, or as if using, scissors.
scissor.example: The erroneous testimony was scissored from the record.

scoff.definition: To jeer; to laugh with contempt and derision.

scorch.definition: To burn the surface of something so as to discolour it

scorn.definition: To feel or display contempt or disdain for something or somebody; to despise.

scotch.definition: To cut or score; to wound superficially.
scotch.example: The rain scotched his plans of going to the beach.

scour.definition: To clean, polish, or wash something by rubbing and scrubbing it vigorously, frequently with an abrasive or cleaning agent.
scour.example: He scoured the burnt food from the pan.

scowl.definition: To wrinkle the brows, as in frowning or displeasure; to put on a frowning look; to look sour, sullen, severe, or angry.
scowl.example: to scowl a rival into submission

scrappy.definition: Consisting of scraps; fragmentary; lacking unity or consistency.

scrawl.definition: To write something hastily or illegibly.

scribble.definition: To write or draw carelessly and in a hurry

scrutinize.definition: To examine something with great care or detail, as to look for hidden or obscure flaws.
scrutinize.example: to scrutinize the conduct or motives of individuals

scuff.definition: To scrape the feet while walking.

sculpt.example: They sculpted a statue out of clay.

scurrilous.definition: (of a person) Given to vulgar verbal abuse; foul-mouthed.
scurrilous.example: We have had our address used by scurrilous crooks in the past to gain assets by fraud.

scurvy.definition: Covered or affected with scurf or scabs; scabby; scurfy; specifically, diseased with the scurvy.
scurvy.example: a scurvy trick; a scurvy knave

seascape.definition: A piece of art that depicts the sea or shoreline.

sedate.definition: (of a person or their behaviour) Remaining composed and dignified, and avoiding too much activity or excitement.

sedentary.definition: Not moving; relatively still; staying in the vicinity.
sedentary.example: The oyster is a sedentary mollusk; the barnacles are sedentary crustaceans.

seduce.definition: To beguile or lure (someone) away from duty, accepted principles, or proper conduct; to lead astray.
seduce.example: He had repeatedly seduced the girl in his car, hotels and his home.

sedulous.definition: Of a person: diligent in application or pursuit; constant and persevering in business or in endeavours to effect a goal; steadily industrious.

seemly.definition: (of behavior) Appropriate; suited to the occasion or purpose; becoming.
seemly.example: His behavior was seemly, as befits a gentleman.

self-absorbed.definition: Overly concerned about oneself, to the point of ignoring the feelings of others.
self-absorbed.example: I don't want to call him self-absorbed but he was more concerned with the scratch on his car than the people in the car he hit.

seine.definition: To use a seine, to fish with a seine.

seminal.definition: Of or relating to seed or semen.
seminal.example: "The Structure of Scientific Revolutions" was a seminal work in the modern philosophy of science.

seminary.definition: Of or relating to seed; seminal.

sentinel.definition: To watch over as a guard.

sepulchral.definition: Relating to a grave or to death; funereal.

sequela.definition: A disease or condition which is caused by an earlier disease or problem.

sequester.definition: To separate from all external influence; to seclude; to withdraw.
sequester.example: The jury was sequestered from the press by the judge's order.

seraph.definition: A six-winged angel; the highest choir or order of angels in Christian angelology, ranked above cherubim, and below God. They are the 5th highest order of angels in Jewish angelology. A detailed description can be found at the beginning of Isaiah chapter 6

serfdom.definition: The state of being a serf.

sermon.definition: Religious discourse; a written or spoken address on a religious or moral matter.

serpentine.definition: Of, pertaining to, or characteristic of snakes.
serpentine.example: The wily criminal was known for his serpentine behavior.

serrate.definition: Having tooth-like projections on one side, as in a saw.
serrate.example: Many click beetles have serrate antennae.

picayune.definition: Petty, trivial; of little consequence; small and of little importance.

pith.definition: To extract the pith from (a plant stem or tree).

plucky.definition: Having or showing pluck, courage or spirit in trying circumstances.

presentiment.definition: A premonition; a feeling that something, often of undesirable nature, is going to happen.

primacy.definition: The state or condition of being prime or first, as in time, place, rank, etc.
primacy.example: December 25 2016, Amruta Patil writing in The Hindu, The book in my hand

prognostication.definition: A statement about or prior knowledge of the future.

pyrrhic.definition: Of or relating to Pyrrhus (319/318–272 BC), Greek general and statesman.
pyrrhic.example: The Pyrrhic army lost the Pyrrhic war.

quisling.definition: A traitor who collaborates with the enemy.

rarefied.definition: Distant from the lives and everyday concerns of ordinary people; esoteric, exclusive, select.
rarefied.example: Philosophical debates can be quite rarefied.

recapitulation.definition: A subsequent brief recitement or enumeration of the major points in a narrative, article, or book.

recrudesce.definition: To recur, or break out anew after a dormant period.

remonstrate.definition: To object; to express disapproval (with, against).

sagacious.definition: Having or showing keen discernment, sound judgment, and farsightedness; mentally shrewd.

sangfroid.definition: Composure, self-possession or imperturbability especially when in a dangerous situation.
sangfroid.example: He handled the stressful situation with great sangfroid.

sardonic.example: He distances himself from people with his nasty, sardonic laughter.

schadenfreude.definition: Malicious enjoyment derived from observing someone else's misfortune.

effacing.definition: To erase (as anything impressed or inscribed upon a surface); to render illegible or indiscernible.
effacing.example: Do not efface what I've written on the chalkboard.

semblance.definition: Likeness, similarity; the quality of being similar.

simulacrum.example: a simulacrum of a New York studio apartment

solicitude.definition: The state of being solicitous; uneasiness of mind occasioned by fear of evil or desire for good; anxiety.

splenetic.definition: Bad-tempered, irritable, peevish, spiteful, habitually angry.

transmute.definition: To change, transform or convert one thing to another, or from one state or form to another.
transmute.example: Did the base metals transmute to gold?

unforthcoming.example: The coach's manner was dour and unforthcoming.

unprepossessing.definition: Unimpressive or unremarkable; dull and ordinary; nondescript.

unstinting.definition: Generous and tireless with one's contributions of time, money, etc.
unstinting.example: We thank her for her unstinting support of our new hospital.

untrammeled.definition: Not limited or restricted; unrestrained; limitless.

unviable.definition: Unable to sustain its own life

vaunted.definition: Highly or widely praised or boasted about.

verisimilitude.definition: The property of seeming true, of resembling reality; resemblance to reality, realism.

vitriol.definition: To subject to bitter verbal abuse.

zeitgeist.definition: The spirit of the age; the taste, outlook, and spirit characteristic of a period.
